1â†’---
     2â†’name: using-git-worktrees
     3â†’description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Using Git Worktrees
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Git worktrees create isolated workspaces sharing the same repository, allowing work on multiple branches simultaneously without switching.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** Systematic directory selection + safety verification = reliable isolation.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Announce at start:** "I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace."
    15â†’
    16â†’## Directory Selection Process
    17â†’
    18â†’Follow this priority order:
    19â†’
    20â†’### 1. Check Existing Directories
    21â†’
    22â†’```bash
    23â†’# Check in priority order
    24â†’ls -d .worktrees 2>/dev/null     # Preferred (hidden)
    25â†’ls -d worktrees 2>/dev/null      # Alternative
    26â†’```
    27â†’
    28â†’**If found:** Use that directory. If both exist, `.worktrees` wins.
    29â†’
    30â†’### 2. Check CLAUDE.md
    31â†’
    32â†’```bash
    33â†’grep -i "worktree.*director" CLAUDE.md 2>/dev/null
    34â†’```
    35â†’
    36â†’**If preference specified:** Use it without asking.
    37â†’
    38â†’### 3. Ask User
    39â†’
    40â†’If no directory exists and no CLAUDE.md preference:
    41â†’
    42â†’```
    43â†’No worktree directory found. Where should I create worktrees?
    44â†’
    45â†’1. .worktrees/ (project-local, hidden)
    46â†’2. ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/<project-name>/ (global location)
    47â†’
    48â†’Which would you prefer?
    49â†’```
    50â†’
    51â†’## Safety Verification
    52â†’
    53â†’### For Project-Local Directories (.worktrees or worktrees)
    54â†’
    55â†’**MUST verify directory is ignored before creating worktree:**
    56â†’
    57â†’```bash
    58â†’# Check if directory is ignored (respects local, global, and system gitignore)
    59â†’git check-ignore -q .worktrees 2>/dev/null || git check-ignore -q worktrees 2>/dev/null
    60â†’```
    61â†’
    62â†’**If NOT ignored:**
    63â†’
    64â†’Per Jesse's rule "Fix broken things immediately":
    65â†’1. Add appropriate line to .gitignore
    66â†’2. Commit the change
    67â†’3. Proceed with worktree creation
    68â†’
    69â†’**Why critical:** Prevents accidentally committing worktree contents to repository.
    70â†’
    71â†’### For Global Directory (~/.config/superpowers/worktrees)
    72â†’
    73â†’No .gitignore verification needed - outside project entirely.
    74â†’
    75â†’## Creation Steps
    76â†’
    77â†’### 1. Detect Project Name
    78â†’
    79â†’```bash
    80â†’project=$(basename "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)")
    81â†’```
    82â†’
    83â†’### 2. Create Worktree
    84â†’
    85â†’```bash
    86â†’# Determine full path
    87â†’case $LOCATION in
    88â†’  .worktrees|worktrees)
    89â†’    path="$LOCATION/$BRANCH_NAME"
    90â†’    ;;
    91â†’  ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/*)
    92â†’    path="~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/$project/$BRANCH_NAME"
    93â†’    ;;
    94â†’esac
    95â†’
    96â†’# Create worktree with new branch
    97â†’git worktree add "$path" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
    98â†’cd "$path"
    99â†’```
   100â†’
   101â†’### 3. Run Project Setup
   102â†’
   103â†’Auto-detect and run appropriate setup:
   104â†’
   105â†’```bash
   106â†’# Node.js
   107â†’if [ -f package.json ]; then npm install; fi
   108â†’
   109â†’# Rust
   110â†’if [ -f Cargo.toml ]; then cargo build; fi
   111â†’
   112â†’# Python
   113â†’if [ -f requirements.txt ]; then pip install -r requirements.txt; fi
   114â†’if [ -f pyproject.toml ]; then poetry install; fi
   115â†’
   116â†’# Go
   117â†’if [ -f go.mod ]; then go mod download; fi
   118â†’```
   119â†’
   120â†’### 4. Verify Clean Baseline
   121â†’
   122â†’Run tests to ensure worktree starts clean:
   123â†’
   124â†’```bash
   125â†’# Examples - use project-appropriate command
   126â†’npm test
   127â†’cargo test
   128â†’pytest
   129â†’go test ./...
   130â†’```
   131â†’
   132â†’**If tests fail:** Report failures, ask whether to proceed or investigate.
   133â†’
   134â†’**If tests pass:** Report ready.
   135â†’
   136â†’### 5. Report Location
   137â†’
   138â†’```
   139â†’Worktree ready at <full-path>
   140â†’Tests passing (<N> tests, 0 failures)
   141â†’Ready to implement <feature-name>
   142â†’```
   143â†’
   144â†’## Quick Reference
   145â†’
   146â†’| Situation | Action |
   147â†’|-----------|--------|
   148â†’| `.worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
   149â†’| `worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
   150â†’| Both exist | Use `.worktrees/` |
   151â†’| Neither exists | Check CLAUDE.md â†’ Ask user |
   152â†’| Directory not ignored | Add to .gitignore + commit |
   153â†’| Tests fail during baseline | Report failures + ask |
   154â†’| No package.json/Cargo.toml | Skip dependency install |
   155â†’
   156â†’## Common Mistakes
   157â†’
   158â†’### Skipping ignore verification
   159â†’
   160â†’- **Problem:** Worktree contents get tracked, pollute git status
   161â†’- **Fix:** Always use `git check-ignore` before creating project-local worktree
   162â†’
   163â†’### Assuming directory location
   164â†’
   165â†’- **Problem:** Creates inconsistency, violates project conventions
   166â†’- **Fix:** Follow priority: existing > CLAUDE.md > ask
   167â†’
   168â†’### Proceeding with failing tests
   169â†’
   170â†’- **Problem:** Can't distinguish new bugs from pre-existing issues
   171â†’- **Fix:** Report failures, get explicit permission to proceed
   172â†’
   173â†’### Hardcoding setup commands
   174â†’
   175â†’- **Problem:** Breaks on projects using different tools
   176â†’- **Fix:** Auto-detect from project files (package.json, etc.)
   177â†’
   178â†’## Example Workflow
   179â†’
   180â†’```
   181â†’You: I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace.
   182â†’
   183â†’[Check .worktrees/ - exists]
   184â†’[Verify ignored - git check-ignore confirms .worktrees/ is ignored]
   185â†’[Create worktree: git worktree add .worktrees/auth -b feature/auth]
   186â†’[Run npm install]
   187â†’[Run npm test - 47 passing]
   188â†’
   189â†’Worktree ready at /Users/jesse/myproject/.worktrees/auth
   190â†’Tests passing (47 tests, 0 failures)
   191â†’Ready to implement auth feature
   192â†’```
   193â†’
   194â†’## Red Flags
   195â†’
   196â†’**Never:**
   197â†’- Create worktree without verifying it's ignored (project-local)
   198â†’- Skip baseline test verification
   199â†’- Proceed with failing tests without asking
   200â†’- Assume directory location when ambiguous
   201â†’- Skip CLAUDE.md check
   202â†’
   203â†’**Always:**
   204â†’- Follow directory priority: existing > CLAUDE.md > ask
   205â†’- Verify directory is ignored for project-local
   206â†’- Auto-detect and run project setup
   207â†’- Verify clean test baseline
   208â†’
   209â†’## Integration
   210â†’
   211â†’**Called by:**
   212â†’- **brainstorming** (Phase 4) - REQUIRED when design is approved and implementation follows
   213â†’- **subagent-driven-development** - REQUIRED before executing any tasks
   214â†’- **executing-plans** - REQUIRED before executing any tasks
   215â†’- Any skill needing isolated workspace
   216â†’
   217â†’**Pairs with:**
   218â†’- **finishing-a-development-branch** - REQUIRED for cleanup after work complete
   219â†’

1â†’---
     2â†’name: test-driven-development
     3â†’description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Test-Driven Development (TDD)
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Write the test first. Watch it fail. Write minimal code to pass.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** If you didn't watch the test fail, you don't know if it tests the right thing.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Violating the letter of the rules is violating the spirit of the rules.**
    15â†’
    16â†’## When to Use
    17â†’
    18â†’**Always:**
    19â†’- New features
    20â†’- Bug fixes
    21â†’- Refactoring
    22â†’- Behavior changes
    23â†’
    24â†’**Exceptions (ask your human partner):**
    25â†’- Throwaway prototypes
    26â†’- Generated code
    27â†’- Configuration files
    28â†’
    29â†’Thinking "skip TDD just this once"? Stop. That's rationalization.
    30â†’
    31â†’## The Iron Law
    32â†’
    33â†’```
    34â†’NO PRODUCTION CODE WITHOUT A FAILING TEST FIRST
    35â†’```
    36â†’
    37â†’Write code before the test? Delete it. Start over.
    38â†’
    39â†’**No exceptions:**
    40â†’- Don't keep it as "reference"
    41â†’- Don't "adapt" it while writing tests
    42â†’- Don't look at it
    43â†’- Delete means delete
    44â†’
    45â†’Implement fresh from tests. Period.
    46â†’
    47â†’## Red-Green-Refactor
    48â†’
    49â†’```dot
    50â†’digraph tdd_cycle {
    51â†’    rankdir=LR;
    52â†’    red [label="RED\nWrite failing test", shape=box, style=filled, fillcolor="#ffcccc"];
    53â†’    verify_red [label="Verify fails\ncorrectly", shape=diamond];
    54â†’    green [label="GREEN\nMinimal code", shape=box, style=filled, fillcolor="#ccffcc"];
    55â†’    verify_green [label="Verify passes\nAll green", shape=diamond];
    56â†’    refactor [label="REFACTOR\nClean up", shape=box, style=filled, fillcolor="#ccccff"];
    57â†’    next [label="Next", shape=ellipse];
    58â†’
    59â†’    red -> verify_red;
    60â†’    verify_red -> green [label="yes"];
    61â†’    verify_red -> red [label="wrong\nfailure"];
    62â†’    green -> verify_green;
    63â†’    verify_green -> refactor [label="yes"];
    64â†’    verify_green -> green [label="no"];
    65â†’    refactor -> verify_green [label="stay\ngreen"];
    66â†’    verify_green -> next;
    67â†’    next -> red;
    68â†’}
    69â†’```
    70â†’
    71â†’### RED - Write Failing Test
    72â†’
    73â†’Write one minimal test showing what should happen.
    74â†’
    75â†’<Good>
    76â†’```typescript
    77â†’test('retries failed operations 3 times', async () => {
    78â†’  let attempts = 0;
    79â†’  const operation = () => {
    80â†’    attempts++;
    81â†’    if (attempts < 3) throw new Error('fail');
    82â†’    return 'success';
    83â†’  };
    84â†’
    85â†’  const result = await retryOperation(operation);
    86â†’
    87â†’  expect(result).toBe('success');
    88â†’  expect(attempts).toBe(3);
    89â†’});
    90â†’```
    91â†’Clear name, tests real behavior, one thing
    92â†’</Good>
    93â†’
    94â†’<Bad>
    95â†’```typescript
    96â†’test('retry works', async () => {
    97â†’  const mock = jest.fn()
    98â†’    .mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error())
    99â†’    .mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error())
   100â†’    .mockResolvedValueOnce('success');
   101â†’  await retryOperation(mock);
   102â†’  expect(mock).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(3);
   103â†’});
   104â†’```
   105â†’Vague name, tests mock not code
   106â†’</Bad>
   107â†’
   108â†’**Requirements:**
   109â†’- One behavior
   110â†’- Clear name
   111â†’- Real code (no mocks unless unavoidable)
   112â†’
   113â†’### Verify RED - Watch It Fail
   114â†’
   115â†’**MANDATORY. Never skip.**
   116â†’
   117â†’```bash
   118â†’npm test path/to/test.test.ts
   119â†’```
   120â†’
   121â†’Confirm:
   122â†’- Test fails (not errors)
   123â†’- Failure message is expected
   124â†’- Fails because feature missing (not typos)
   125â†’
   126â†’**Test passes?** You're testing existing behavior. Fix test.
   127â†’
   128â†’**Test errors?** Fix error, re-run until it fails correctly.
   129â†’
   130â†’### GREEN - Minimal Code
   131â†’
   132â†’Write simplest code to pass the test.
   133â†’
   134â†’<Good>
   135â†’```typescript
   136â†’async function retryOperation<T>(fn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
   137â†’  for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
   138â†’    try {
   139â†’      return await fn();
   140â†’    } catch (e) {
   141â†’      if (i === 2) throw e;
   142â†’    }
   143â†’  }
   144â†’  throw new Error('unreachable');
   145â†’}
   146â†’```
   147â†’Just enough to pass
   148â†’</Good>
   149â†’
   150â†’<Bad>
   151â†’```typescript
   152â†’async function retryOperation<T>(
   153â†’  fn: () => Promise<T>,
   154â†’  options?: {
   155â†’    maxRetries?: number;
   156â†’    backoff?: 'linear' | 'exponential';
   157â†’    onRetry?: (attempt: number) => void;
   158â†’  }
   159â†’): Promise<T> {
   160â†’  // YAGNI
   161â†’}
   162â†’```
   163â†’Over-engineered
   164â†’</Bad>
   165â†’
   166â†’Don't add features, refactor other code, or "improve" beyond the test.
   167â†’
   168â†’### Verify GREEN - Watch It Pass
   169â†’
   170â†’**MANDATORY.**
   171â†’
   172â†’```bash
   173â†’npm test path/to/test.test.ts
   174â†’```
   175â†’
   176â†’Confirm:
   177â†’- Test passes
   178â†’- Other tests still pass
   179â†’- Output pristine (no errors, warnings)
   180â†’
   181â†’**Test fails?** Fix code, not test.
   182â†’
   183â†’**Other tests fail?** Fix now.
   184â†’
   185â†’### REFACTOR - Clean Up
   186â†’
   187â†’After green only:
   188â†’- Remove duplication
   189â†’- Improve names
   190â†’- Extract helpers
   191â†’
   192â†’Keep tests green. Don't add behavior.
   193â†’
   194â†’### Repeat
   195â†’
   196â†’Next failing test for next feature.
   197â†’
   198â†’## Good Tests
   199â†’
   200â†’| Quality | Good | Bad |
   201â†’|---------|------|-----|
   202â†’| **Minimal** | One thing. "and" in name? Split it. | `test('validates email and domain and whitespace')` |
   203â†’| **Clear** | Name describes behavior | `test('test1')` |
   204â†’| **Shows intent** | Demonstrates desired API | Obscures what code should do |
   205â†’
   206â†’## Why Order Matters
   207â†’
   208â†’**"I'll write tests after to verify it works"**
   209â†’
   210â†’Tests written after code pass immediately. Passing immediately proves nothing:
   211â†’- Might test wrong thing
   212â†’- Might test implementation, not behavior
   213â†’- Might miss edge cases you forgot
   214â†’- You never saw it catch the bug
   215â†’
   216â†’Test-first forces you to see the test fail, proving it actually tests something.
   217â†’
   218â†’**"I already manually tested all the edge cases"**
   219â†’
   220â†’Manual testing is ad-hoc. You think you tested everything but:
   221â†’- No record of what you tested
   222â†’- Can't re-run when code changes
   223â†’- Easy to forget cases under pressure
   224â†’- "It worked when I tried it" â‰  comprehensive
   225â†’
   226â†’Automated tests are systematic. They run the same way every time.
   227â†’
   228â†’**"Deleting X hours of work is wasteful"**
   229â†’
   230â†’Sunk cost fallacy. The time is already gone. Your choice now:
   231â†’- Delete and rewrite with TDD (X more hours, high confidence)
   232â†’- Keep it and add tests after (30 min, low confidence, likely bugs)
   233â†’
   234â†’The "waste" is keeping code you can't trust. Working code without real tests is technical debt.
   235â†’
   236â†’**"TDD is dogmatic, being pragmatic means adapting"**
   237â†’
   238â†’TDD IS pragmatic:
   239â†’- Finds bugs before commit (faster than debugging after)
   240â†’- Prevents regressions (tests catch breaks immediately)
   241â†’- Documents behavior (tests show how to use code)
   242â†’- Enables refactoring (change freely, tests catch breaks)
   243â†’
   244â†’"Pragmatic" shortcuts = debugging in production = slower.
   245â†’
   246â†’**"Tests after achieve the same goals - it's spirit not ritual"**
   247â†’
   248â†’No. Tests-after answer "What does this do?" Tests-first answer "What should this do?"
   249â†’
   250â†’Tests-after are biased by your implementation. You test what you built, not what's required. You verify remembered edge cases, not discovered ones.
   251â†’
   252â†’Tests-first force edge case discovery before implementing. Tests-after verify you remembered everything (you didn't).
   253â†’
   254â†’30 minutes of tests after â‰  TDD. You get coverage, lose proof tests work.
   255â†’
   256â†’## Common Rationalizations
   257â†’
   258â†’| Excuse | Reality |
   259â†’|--------|---------|
   260â†’| "Too simple to test" | Simple code breaks. Test takes 30 seconds. |
   261â†’| "I'll test after" | Tests passing immediately prove nothing. |
   262â†’| "Tests after achieve same goals" | Tests-after = "what does this do?" Tests-first = "what should this do?" |
   263â†’| "Already manually tested" | Ad-hoc â‰  systematic. No record, can't re-run. |
   264â†’| "Deleting X hours is wasteful" | Sunk cost fallacy. Keeping unverified code is technical debt. |
   265â†’| "Keep as reference, write tests first" | You'll adapt it. That's testing after. Delete means delete. |
   266â†’| "Need to explore first" | Fine. Throw away exploration, start with TDD. |
   267â†’| "Test hard = design unclear" | Listen to test. Hard to test = hard to use. |
   268â†’| "TDD will slow me down" | TDD faster than debugging. Pragmatic = test-first. |
   269â†’| "Manual test faster" | Manual doesn't prove edge cases. You'll re-test every change. |
   270â†’| "Existing code has no tests" | You're improving it. Add tests for existing code. |
   271â†’
   272â†’## Red Flags - STOP and Start Over
   273â†’
   274â†’- Code before test
   275â†’- Test after implementation
   276â†’- Test passes immediately
   277â†’- Can't explain why test failed
   278â†’- Tests added "later"
   279â†’- Rationalizing "just this once"
   280â†’- "I already manually tested it"
   281â†’- "Tests after achieve the same purpose"
   282â†’- "It's about spirit not ritual"
   283â†’- "Keep as reference" or "adapt existing code"
   284â†’- "Already spent X hours, deleting is wasteful"
   285â†’- "TDD is dogmatic, I'm being pragmatic"
   286â†’- "This is different because..."
   287â†’
   288â†’**All of these mean: Delete code. Start over with TDD.**
   289â†’
   290â†’## Example: Bug Fix
   291â†’
   292â†’**Bug:** Empty email accepted
   293â†’
   294â†’**RED**
   295â†’```typescript
   296â†’test('rejects empty email', async () => {
   297â†’  const result = await submitForm({ email: '' });
   298â†’  expect(result.error).toBe('Email required');
   299â†’});
   300â†’```
   301â†’
   302â†’**Verify RED**
   303â†’```bash
   304â†’$ npm test
   305â†’FAIL: expected 'Email required', got undefined
   306â†’```
   307â†’
   308â†’**GREEN**
   309â†’```typescript
   310â†’function submitForm(data: FormData) {
   311â†’  if (!data.email?.trim()) {
   312â†’    return { error: 'Email required' };
   313â†’  }
   314â†’  // ...
   315â†’}
   316â†’```
   317â†’
   318â†’**Verify GREEN**
   319â†’```bash
   320â†’$ npm test
   321â†’PASS
   322â†’```
   323â†’
   324â†’**REFACTOR**
   325â†’Extract validation for multiple fields if needed.
   326â†’
   327â†’## Verification Checklist
   328â†’
   329â†’Before marking work complete:
   330â†’
   331â†’- [ ] Every new function/method has a test
   332â†’- [ ] Watched each test fail before implementing
   333â†’- [ ] Each test failed for expected reason (feature missing, not typo)
   334â†’- [ ] Wrote minimal code to pass each test
   335â†’- [ ] All tests pass
   336â†’- [ ] Output pristine (no errors, warnings)
   337â†’- [ ] Tests use real code (mocks only if unavoidable)
   338â†’- [ ] Edge cases and errors covered
   339â†’
   340â†’Can't check all boxes? You skipped TDD. Start over.
   341â†’
   342â†’## When Stuck
   343â†’
   344â†’| Problem | Solution |
   345â†’|---------|----------|
   346â†’| Don't know how to test | Write wished-for API. Write assertion first. Ask your human partner. |
   347â†’| Test too complicated | Design too complicated. Simplify interface. |
   348â†’| Must mock everything | Code too coupled. Use dependency injection. |
   349â†’| Test setup huge | Extract helpers. Still complex? Simplify design. |
   350â†’
   351â†’## Debugging Integration
   352â†’
   353â†’Bug found? Write failing test reproducing it. Follow TDD cycle. Test proves fix and prevents regression.
   354â†’
   355â†’Never fix bugs without a test.
   356â†’
   357â†’## Testing Anti-Patterns
   358â†’
   359â†’When adding mocks or test utilities, read @testing-anti-patterns.md to avoid common pitfalls:
   360â†’- Testing mock behavior instead of real behavior
   361â†’- Adding test-only methods to production classes
   362â†’- Mocking without understanding dependencies
   363â†’
   364â†’## Final Rule
   365â†’
   366â†’```
   367â†’Production code â†’ test exists and failed first
   368â†’Otherwise â†’ not TDD
   369â†’```
   370â†’
   371â†’No exceptions without your human partner's permission.
   372â†’

     2â†’name: systematic-debugging
     3â†’description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Systematic Debugging
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Random fixes waste time and create new bugs. Quick patches mask underlying issues.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** ALWAYS find root cause before attempting fixes. Symptom fixes are failure.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Violating the letter of this process is violating the spirit of debugging.**
    15â†’
    16â†’## The Iron Law
    17â†’
    18â†’```
    19â†’NO FIXES WITHOUT ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION FIRST
    20â†’```
    21â†’
    22â†’If you haven't completed Phase 1, you cannot propose fixes.
    23â†’
    24â†’## When to Use
    25â†’
    26â†’Use for ANY technical issue:
    27â†’- Test failures
    28â†’- Bugs in production
    29â†’- Unexpected behavior
    30â†’- Performance problems
    31â†’- Build failures
    32â†’- Integration issues
    33â†’
    34â†’**Use this ESPECIALLY when:**
    35â†’- Under time pressure (emergencies make guessing tempting)
    36â†’- "Just one quick fix" seems obvious
    37â†’- You've already tried multiple fixes
    38â†’- Previous fix didn't work
    39â†’- You don't fully understand the issue
    40â†’
    41â†’**Don't skip when:**
    42â†’- Issue seems simple (simple bugs have root causes too)
    43â†’- You're in a hurry (rushing guarantees rework)
    44â†’- Manager wants it fixed NOW (systematic is faster than thrashing)
    45â†’
    46â†’## The Four Phases
    47â†’
    48â†’You MUST complete each phase before proceeding to the next.
    49â†’
    50â†’### Phase 1: Root Cause Investigation
    51â†’
    52â†’**BEFORE attempting ANY fix:**
    53â†’
    54â†’1. **Read Error Messages Carefully**
    55â†’   - Don't skip past errors or warnings
    56â†’   - They often contain the exact solution
    57â†’   - Read stack traces completely
    58â†’   - Note line numbers, file paths, error codes
    59â†’
    60â†’2. **Reproduce Consistently**
    61â†’   - Can you trigger it reliably?
    62â†’   - What are the exact steps?
    63â†’   - Does it happen every time?
    64â†’   - If not reproducible â†’ gather more data, don't guess
    65â†’
    66â†’3. **Check Recent Changes**
    67â†’   - What changed that could cause this?
    68â†’   - Git diff, recent commits
    69â†’   - New dependencies, config changes
    70â†’   - Environmental differences
    71â†’
    72â†’4. **Gather Evidence in Multi-Component Systems**
    73â†’
    74â†’   **WHEN system has multiple components (CI â†’ build â†’ signing, API â†’ service â†’ database):**
    75â†’
    76â†’   **BEFORE proposing fixes, add diagnostic instrumentation:**
    77â†’   ```
    78â†’   For EACH component boundary:
    79â†’     - Log what data enters component
    80â†’     - Log what data exits component
    81â†’     - Verify environment/config propagation
    82â†’     - Check state at each layer
    83â†’
    84â†’   Run once to gather evidence showing WHERE it breaks
    85â†’   THEN analyze evidence to identify failing component
    86â†’   THEN investigate that specific component
    87â†’   ```
    88â†’
    89â†’   **Example (multi-layer system):**
    90â†’   ```bash
    91â†’   # Layer 1: Workflow
    92â†’   echo "=== Secrets available in workflow: ==="
    93â†’   echo "IDENTITY: ${IDENTITY:+SET}${IDENTITY:-UNSET}"
    94â†’
    95â†’   # Layer 2: Build script
    96â†’   echo "=== Env vars in build script: ==="
    97â†’   env | grep IDENTITY || echo "IDENTITY not in environment"
    98â†’
    99â†’   # Layer 3: Signing script
   100â†’   echo "=== Keychain state: ==="
   101â†’   security list-keychains
   102â†’   security find-identity -v
   103â†’
   104â†’   # Layer 4: Actual signing
   105â†’   codesign --sign "$IDENTITY" --verbose=4 "$APP"
   106â†’   ```
   107â†’
   108â†’   **This reveals:** Which layer fails (secrets â†’ workflow âś“, workflow â†’ build âś—)
   109â†’
   110â†’5. **Trace Data Flow**
   111â†’
   112â†’   **WHEN error is deep in call stack:**
   113â†’
   114â†’   See `root-cause-tracing.md` in this directory for the complete backward tracing technique.
   115â†’
   116â†’   **Quick version:**
   117â†’   - Where does bad value originate?
   118â†’   - What called this with bad value?
   119â†’   - Keep tracing up until you find the source
   120â†’   - Fix at source, not at symptom
   121â†’
   122â†’### Phase 2: Pattern Analysis
   123â†’
   124â†’**Find the pattern before fixing:**
   125â†’
   126â†’1. **Find Working Examples**
   127â†’   - Locate similar working code in same codebase
   128â†’   - What works that's similar to what's broken?
   129â†’
   130â†’2. **Compare Against References**
   131â†’   - If implementing pattern, read reference implementation COMPLETELY
   132â†’   - Don't skim - read every line
   133â†’   - Understand the pattern fully before applying
   134â†’
   135â†’3. **Identify Differences**
   136â†’   - What's different between working and broken?
   137â†’   - List every difference, however small
   138â†’   - Don't assume "that can't matter"
   139â†’
   140â†’4. **Understand Dependencies**
   141â†’   - What other components does this need?
   142â†’   - What settings, config, environment?
   143â†’   - What assumptions does it make?
   144â†’
   145â†’### Phase 3: Hypothesis and Testing
   146â†’
   147â†’**Scientific method:**
   148â†’
   149â†’1. **Form Single Hypothesis**
   150â†’   - State clearly: "I think X is the root cause because Y"
   151â†’   - Write it down
   152â†’   - Be specific, not vague
   153â†’
   154â†’2. **Test Minimally**
   155â†’   - Make the SMALLEST possible change to test hypothesis
   156â†’   - One variable at a time
   157â†’   - Don't fix multiple things at once
   158â†’
   159â†’3. **Verify Before Continuing**
   160â†’   - Did it work? Yes â†’ Phase 4
   161â†’   - Didn't work? Form NEW hypothesis
   162â†’   - DON'T add more fixes on top
   163â†’
   164â†’4. **When You Don't Know**
   165â†’   - Say "I don't understand X"
   166â†’   - Don't pretend to know
   167â†’   - Ask for help
   168â†’   - Research more
   169â†’
   170â†’### Phase 4: Implementation
   171â†’
   172â†’**Fix the root cause, not the symptom:**
   173â†’
   174â†’1. **Create Failing Test Case**
   175â†’   - Simplest possible reproduction
   176â†’   - Automated test if possible
   177â†’   - One-off test script if no framework
   178â†’   - MUST have before fixing
   179â†’   - Use the `superpowers:test-driven-development` skill for writing proper failing tests
   180â†’
   181â†’2. **Implement Single Fix**
   182â†’   - Address the root cause identified
   183â†’   - ONE change at a time
   184â†’   - No "while I'm here" improvements
   185â†’   - No bundled refactoring
   186â†’
   187â†’3. **Verify Fix**
   188â†’   - Test passes now?
   189â†’   - No other tests broken?
   190â†’   - Issue actually resolved?
   191â†’
   192â†’4. **If Fix Doesn't Work**
   193â†’   - STOP
   194â†’   - Count: How many fixes have you tried?
   195â†’   - If < 3: Return to Phase 1, re-analyze with new information
   196â†’   - **If â‰Ą 3: STOP and question the architecture (step 5 below)**
   197â†’   - DON'T attempt Fix #4 without architectural discussion
   198â†’
   199â†’5. **If 3+ Fixes Failed: Question Architecture**
   200â†’
   201â†’   **Pattern indicating architectural problem:**
   202â†’   - Each fix reveals new shared state/coupling/problem in different place
   203â†’   - Fixes require "massive refactoring" to implement
   204â†’   - Each fix creates new symptoms elsewhere
   205â†’
   206â†’   **STOP and question fundamentals:**
   207â†’   - Is this pattern fundamentally sound?
   208â†’   - Are we "sticking with it through sheer inertia"?
   209â†’   - Should we refactor architecture vs. continue fixing symptoms?
   210â†’
   211â†’   **Discuss with your human partner before attempting more fixes**
   212â†’
   213â†’   This is NOT a failed hypothesis - this is a wrong architecture.
   214â†’
   215â†’## Red Flags - STOP and Follow Process
   216â†’
   217â†’If you catch yourself thinking:
   218â†’- "Quick fix for now, investigate later"
   219â†’- "Just try changing X and see if it works"
   220â†’- "Add multiple changes, run tests"
   221â†’- "Skip the test, I'll manually verify"
   222â†’- "It's probably X, let me fix that"
   223â†’- "I don't fully understand but this might work"
   224â†’- "Pattern says X but I'll adapt it differently"
   225â†’- "Here are the main problems: [lists fixes without investigation]"
   226â†’- Proposing solutions before tracing data flow
   227â†’- **"One more fix attempt" (when already tried 2+)**
   228â†’- **Each fix reveals new problem in different place**
   229â†’
   230â†’**ALL of these mean: STOP. Return to Phase 1.**
   231â†’
   232â†’**If 3+ fixes failed:** Question the architecture (see Phase 4.5)
   233â†’
   234â†’## your human partner's Signals You're Doing It Wrong
   235â†’
   236â†’**Watch for these redirections:**
   237â†’- "Is that not happening?" - You assumed without verifying
   238â†’- "Will it show us...?" - You should have added evidence gathering
   239â†’- "Stop guessing" - You're proposing fixes without understanding
   240â†’- "Ultrathink this" - Question fundamentals, not just symptoms
   241â†’- "We're stuck?" (frustrated) - Your approach isn't working
   242â†’
   243â†’**When you see these:** STOP. Return to Phase 1.
   244â†’
   245â†’## Common Rationalizations
   246â†’
   247â†’| Excuse | Reality |
   248â†’|--------|---------|
   249â†’| "Issue is simple, don't need process" | Simple issues have root causes too. Process is fast for simple bugs. |
   250â†’| "Emergency, no time for process" | Systematic debugging is FASTER than guess-and-check thrashing. |
   251â†’| "Just try this first, then investigate" | First fix sets the pattern. Do it right from the start. |
   252â†’| "I'll write test after confirming fix works" | Untested fixes don't stick. Test first proves it. |
   253â†’| "Multiple fixes at once saves time" | Can't isolate what worked. Causes new bugs. |
   254â†’| "Reference too long, I'll adapt the pattern" | Partial understanding guarantees bugs. Read it completely. |
   255â†’| "I see the problem, let me fix it" | Seeing symptoms â‰  understanding root cause. |
   256â†’| "One more fix attempt" (after 2+ failures) | 3+ failures = architectural problem. Question pattern, don't fix again. |
   257â†’
   258â†’## Quick Reference
   259â†’
   260â†’| Phase | Key Activities | Success Criteria |
   261â†’|-------|---------------|------------------|
   262â†’| **1. Root Cause** | Read errors, reproduce, check changes, gather evidence | Understand WHAT and WHY |
   263â†’| **2. Pattern** | Find working examples, compare | Identify differences |
   264â†’| **3. Hypothesis** | Form theory, test minimally | Confirmed or new hypothesis |
   265â†’| **4. Implementation** | Create test, fix, verify | Bug resolved, tests pass |
   266â†’
   267â†’## When Process Reveals "No Root Cause"
   268â†’
   269â†’If systematic investigation reveals issue is truly environmental, timing-dependent, or external:
   270â†’
   271â†’1. You've completed the process
   272â†’2. Document what you investigated
   273â†’3. Implement appropriate handling (retry, timeout, error message)
   274â†’4. Add monitoring/logging for future investigation
   275â†’
   276â†’**But:** 95% of "no root cause" cases are incomplete investigation.
   277â†’
   278â†’## Supporting Techniques
   279â†’
   280â†’These techniques are part of systematic debugging and available in this directory:
   281â†’
   282â†’- **`root-cause-tracing.md`** - Trace bugs backward through call stack to find original trigger
   283â†’- **`defense-in-depth.md`** - Add validation at multiple layers after finding root cause
   284â†’- **`condition-based-waiting.md`** - Replace arbitrary timeouts with condition polling
   285â†’
   286â†’**Related skills:**
   287â†’- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - For creating failing test case (Phase 4, Step 1)
   288â†’- **superpowers:verification-before-completion** - Verify fix worked before claiming success
   289â†’
   290â†’## Real-World Impact
   291â†’
   292â†’From debugging sessions:
   293â†’- Systematic approach: 15-30 minutes to fix
   294â†’- Random fixes approach: 2-3 hours of thrashing
   295â†’- First-time fix rate: 95% vs 40%
   296â†’- New bugs introduced: Near zero vs common
   297â†’

    82â†’| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
    83â†’| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
    84â†’| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
    85â†’| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
    86â†’| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
    87â†’| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
    88â†’| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
    89â†’| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
    90â†’| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
    91â†’| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
    92â†’| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
    93â†’| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept â‰  using the skill. Invoke it. |
    94â†’
    95â†’## Skill Priority
    96â†’
    97â†’When multiple skills could apply, use this order:
    98â†’
    99â†’1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
   100â†’2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution
   101â†’
   102â†’"Let's build X" â†’ brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
   103â†’"Fix this bug" â†’ debugging first, then domain-specific skills.
   104â†’
   105â†’## Skill Types
   106â†’
   107â†’**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.
   108â†’
   109â†’**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.
   110â†’
   111â†’The skill itself tells you which.
   112â†’
   113â†’## User Instructions
   114â†’
   115â†’Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.
   116â†’

     2â†’name: dispatching-parallel-agents
     3â†’description: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Dispatching Parallel Agents
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history â€” you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.
    11â†’
    12â†’When you have multiple unrelated failures (different test files, different subsystems, different bugs), investigating them sequentially wastes time. Each investigation is independent and can happen in parallel.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Core principle:** Dispatch one agent per independent problem domain. Let them work concurrently.
    15â†’
    16â†’## When to Use
    17â†’
    18â†’```dot
    19â†’digraph when_to_use {
    20â†’    "Multiple failures?" [shape=diamond];
    21â†’    "Are they independent?" [shape=diamond];
    22â†’    "Single agent investigates all" [shape=box];
    23â†’    "One agent per problem domain" [shape=box];
    24â†’    "Can they work in parallel?" [shape=diamond];
    25â†’    "Sequential agents" [shape=box];
    26â†’    "Parallel dispatch" [shape=box];
    27â†’
    28â†’    "Multiple failures?" -> "Are they independent?" [label="yes"];
    29â†’    "Are they independent?" -> "Single agent investigates all" [label="no - related"];
    30â†’    "Are they independent?" -> "Can they work in parallel?" [label="yes"];
    31â†’    "Can they work in parallel?" -> "Parallel dispatch" [label="yes"];
    32â†’    "Can they work in parallel?" -> "Sequential agents" [label="no - shared state"];
    33â†’}
    34â†’```
    35â†’
    36â†’**Use when:**
    37â†’- 3+ test files failing with different root causes
    38â†’- Multiple subsystems broken independently
    39â†’- Each problem can be understood without context from others
    40â†’- No shared state between investigations
    41â†’
    42â†’**Don't use when:**
    43â†’- Failures are related (fix one might fix others)
    44â†’- Need to understand full system state
    45â†’- Agents would interfere with each other
    46â†’
    47â†’## The Pattern
    48â†’
    49â†’### 1. Identify Independent Domains
    50â†’
    51â†’Group failures by what's broken:
    52â†’- File A tests: Tool approval flow
    53â†’- File B tests: Batch completion behavior
    54â†’- File C tests: Abort functionality
    55â†’
    56â†’Each domain is independent - fixing tool approval doesn't affect abort tests.
    57â†’
    58â†’### 2. Create Focused Agent Tasks
    59â†’
    60â†’Each agent gets:
    61â†’- **Specific scope:** One test file or subsystem
    62â†’- **Clear goal:** Make these tests pass
    63â†’- **Constraints:** Don't change other code
    64â†’- **Expected output:** Summary of what you found and fixed
    65â†’
    66â†’### 3. Dispatch in Parallel
    67â†’
    68â†’```typescript
    69â†’// In Claude Code / AI environment
    70â†’Task("Fix agent-tool-abort.test.ts failures")
    71â†’Task("Fix batch-completion-behavior.test.ts failures")
    72â†’Task("Fix tool-approval-race-conditions.test.ts failures")
    73â†’// All three run concurrently
    74â†’```
    75â†’
    76â†’### 4. Review and Integrate
    77â†’
    78â†’When agents return:
    79â†’- Read each summary
    80â†’- Verify fixes don't conflict
    81â†’- Run full test suite
    82â†’- Integrate all changes
    83â†’
    84â†’## Agent Prompt Structure
    85â†’
    86â†’Good agent prompts are:
    87â†’1. **Focused** - One clear problem domain
    88â†’2. **Self-contained** - All context needed to understand the problem
    89â†’3. **Specific about output** - What should the agent return?
    90â†’
    91â†’```markdown
    92â†’Fix the 3 failing tests in src/agents/agent-tool-abort.test.ts:
    93â†’
    94â†’1. "should abort tool with partial output capture" - expects 'interrupted at' in message
    95â†’2. "should handle mixed completed and aborted tools" - fast tool aborted instead of completed
    96â†’3. "should properly track pendingToolCount" - expects 3 results but gets 0
    97â†’
    98â†’These are timing/race condition issues. Your task:
    99â†’
   100â†’1. Read the test file and understand what each test verifies
   101â†’2. Identify root cause - timing issues or actual bugs?
   102â†’3. Fix by:
   103â†’   - Replacing arbitrary timeouts with event-based waiting
   104â†’   - Fixing bugs in abort implementation if found
   105â†’   - Adjusting test expectations if testing changed behavior
   106â†’
   107â†’Do NOT just increase timeouts - find the real issue.
   108â†’
   109â†’Return: Summary of what you found and what you fixed.
   110â†’```
   111â†’
   112â†’## Common Mistakes
   113â†’
   114â†’**âťŚ Too broad:** "Fix all the tests" - agent gets lost
   115â†’**âś… Specific:** "Fix agent-tool-abort.test.ts" - focused scope
   116â†’
   117â†’**âťŚ No context:** "Fix the race condition" - agent doesn't know where
   118â†’**âś… Context:** Paste the error messages and test names
   119â†’
   120â†’**âťŚ No constraints:** Agent might refactor everything
   121â†’**âś… Constraints:** "Do NOT change production code" or "Fix tests only"
   122â†’
   123â†’**âťŚ Vague output:** "Fix it" - you don't know what changed
   124â†’**âś… Specific:** "Return summary of root cause and changes"
   125â†’
   126â†’## When NOT to Use
   127â†’
   128â†’**Related failures:** Fixing one might fix others - investigate together first
   129â†’**Need full context:** Understanding requires seeing entire system
   130â†’**Exploratory debugging:** You don't know what's broken yet
   131â†’**Shared state:** Agents would interfere (editing same files, using same resources)
   132â†’
   133â†’## Real Example from Session
   134â†’
   135â†’**Scenario:** 6 test failures across 3 files after major refactoring
   136â†’
   137â†’**Failures:**
   138â†’- agent-tool-abort.test.ts: 3 failures (timing issues)
   139â†’- batch-completion-behavior.test.ts: 2 failures (tools not executing)
   140â†’- tool-approval-race-conditions.test.ts: 1 failure (execution count = 0)
   141â†’
   142â†’**Decision:** Independent domains - abort logic separate from batch completion separate from race conditions
   143â†’
   144â†’**Dispatch:**
   145â†’```
   146â†’Agent 1 â†’ Fix agent-tool-abort.test.ts
   147â†’Agent 2 â†’ Fix batch-completion-behavior.test.ts
   148â†’Agent 3 â†’ Fix tool-approval-race-conditions.test.ts
   149â†’```
   150â†’
   151â†’**Results:**
   152â†’- Agent 1: Replaced timeouts with event-based waiting
   153â†’- Agent 2: Fixed event structure bug (threadId in wrong place)
   154â†’- Agent 3: Added wait for async tool execution to complete
   155â†’
   156â†’**Integration:** All fixes independent, no conflicts, full suite green
   157â†’
   158â†’**Time saved:** 3 problems solved in parallel vs sequentially
   159â†’
   160â†’## Key Benefits
   161â†’
   162â†’1. **Parallelization** - Multiple investigations happen simultaneously
   163â†’2. **Focus** - Each agent has narrow scope, less context to track
   164â†’3. **Independence** - Agents don't interfere with each other
   165â†’4. **Speed** - 3 problems solved in time of 1
   166â†’
   167â†’## Verification
   168â†’
   169â†’After agents return:
   170â†’1. **Review each summary** - Understand what changed
   171â†’2. **Check for conflicts** - Did agents edit same code?
   172â†’3. **Run full suite** - Verify all fixes work together
   173â†’4. **Spot check** - Agents can make systematic errors
   174â†’
   175â†’## Real-World Impact
   176â†’
   177â†’From debugging session (2025-10-03):
   178â†’- 6 failures across 3 files
   179â†’- 3 agents dispatched in parallel
   180â†’- All investigations completed concurrently
   181â†’- All fixes integrated successfully
   182â†’- Zero conflicts between agent changes
   183â†’

     2â†’name: executing-plans
     3â†’description: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Executing Plans
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Load plan, review critically, execute all tasks, report when complete.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Announce at start:** "I'm using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan."
    13â†’
    14â†’**Note:** Tell your human partner that Superpowers works much better with access to subagents. The quality of its work will be significantly higher if run on a platform with subagent support (such as Claude Code or Codex). If subagents are available, use superpowers:subagent-driven-development instead of this skill.
    15â†’
    16â†’## The Process
    17â†’
    18â†’### Step 1: Load and Review Plan
    19â†’1. Read plan file
    20â†’2. Review critically - identify any questions or concerns about the plan
    21â†’3. If concerns: Raise them with your human partner before starting
    22â†’4. If no concerns: Create TodoWrite and proceed
    23â†’
    24â†’### Step 2: Execute Tasks
    25â†’
    26â†’For each task:
    27â†’1. Mark as in_progress
    28â†’2. Follow each step exactly (plan has bite-sized steps)
    29â†’3. Run verifications as specified
    30â†’4. Mark as completed
    31â†’
    32â†’### Step 3: Complete Development
    33â†’
    34â†’After all tasks complete and verified:
    35â†’- Announce: "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."
    36â†’- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
    37â†’- Follow that skill to verify tests, present options, execute choice
    38â†’
    39â†’## When to Stop and Ask for Help
    40â†’
    41â†’**STOP executing immediately when:**
    42â†’- Hit a blocker (missing dependency, test fails, instruction unclear)
    43â†’- Plan has critical gaps preventing starting
    44â†’- You don't understand an instruction
    45â†’- Verification fails repeatedly
    46â†’
    47â†’**Ask for clarification rather than guessing.**
    48â†’
    49â†’## When to Revisit Earlier Steps
    50â†’
    51â†’**Return to Review (Step 1) when:**
    52â†’- Partner updates the plan based on your feedback
    53â†’- Fundamental approach needs rethinking
    54â†’
    55â†’**Don't force through blockers** - stop and ask.
    56â†’
    57â†’## Remember
    58â†’- Review plan critically first
    59â†’- Follow plan steps exactly
    60â†’- Don't skip verifications
    61â†’- Reference skills when plan says to
    62â†’- Stop when blocked, don't guess
    63â†’- Never start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
    64â†’
    65â†’## Integration
    66â†’
    67â†’**Required workflow skills:**
    68â†’- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
    69â†’- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
    70â†’- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks
    71â†’

     2â†’name: finishing-a-development-branch
     3â†’description: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Finishing a Development Branch
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Guide completion of development work by presenting clear options and handling chosen workflow.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** Verify tests â†’ Present options â†’ Execute choice â†’ Clean up.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Announce at start:** "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."
    15â†’
    16â†’## The Process
    17â†’
    18â†’### Step 1: Verify Tests
    19â†’
    20â†’**Before presenting options, verify tests pass:**
    21â†’
    22â†’```bash
    23â†’# Run project's test suite
    24â†’npm test / cargo test / pytest / go test ./...
    25â†’```
    26â†’
    27â†’**If tests fail:**
    28â†’```
    29â†’Tests failing (<N> failures). Must fix before completing:
    30â†’
    31â†’[Show failures]
    32â†’
    33â†’Cannot proceed with merge/PR until tests pass.
    34â†’```
    35â†’
    36â†’Stop. Don't proceed to Step 2.
    37â†’
    38â†’**If tests pass:** Continue to Step 2.
    39â†’
    40â†’### Step 2: Determine Base Branch
    41â†’
    42â†’```bash
    43â†’# Try common base branches
    44â†’git merge-base HEAD main 2>/dev/null || git merge-base HEAD master 2>/dev/null
    45â†’```
    46â†’
    47â†’Or ask: "This branch split from main - is that correct?"
    48â†’
    49â†’### Step 3: Present Options
    50â†’
    51â†’Present exactly these 4 options:
    52â†’
    53â†’```
    54â†’Implementation complete. What would you like to do?
    55â†’
    56â†’1. Merge back to <base-branch> locally
    57â†’2. Push and create a Pull Request
    58â†’3. Keep the branch as-is (I'll handle it later)
    59â†’4. Discard this work
    60â†’
    61â†’Which option?
    62â†’```
    63â†’
    64â†’**Don't add explanation** - keep options concise.
    65â†’
    66â†’### Step 4: Execute Choice
    67â†’
    68â†’#### Option 1: Merge Locally
    69â†’
    70â†’```bash
    71â†’# Switch to base branch
    72â†’git checkout <base-branch>
    73â†’
    74â†’# Pull latest
    75â†’git pull
    76â†’
    77â†’# Merge feature branch
    78â†’git merge <feature-branch>
    79â†’
    80â†’# Verify tests on merged result
    81â†’<test command>
    82â†’
    83â†’# If tests pass
    84â†’git branch -d <feature-branch>
    85â†’```
    86â†’
    87â†’Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 5)
    88â†’
    89â†’#### Option 2: Push and Create PR
    90â†’
    91â†’```bash
    92â†’# Push branch
    93â†’git push -u origin <feature-branch>
    94â†’
    95â†’# Create PR
    96â†’gh pr create --title "<title>" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
    97â†’## Summary
    98â†’<2-3 bullets of what changed>
    99â†’
   100â†’## Test Plan
   101â†’- [ ] <verification steps>
   102â†’EOF
   103â†’)"
   104â†’```
   105â†’
   106â†’Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 5)
   107â†’
   108â†’#### Option 3: Keep As-Is
   109â†’
   110â†’Report: "Keeping branch <name>. Worktree preserved at <path>."
   111â†’
   112â†’**Don't cleanup worktree.**
   113â†’
   114â†’#### Option 4: Discard
   115â†’
   116â†’**Confirm first:**
   117â†’```
   118â†’This will permanently delete:
   119â†’- Branch <name>
   120â†’- All commits: <commit-list>
   121â†’- Worktree at <path>
   122â†’
   123â†’Type 'discard' to confirm.
   124â†’```
   125â†’
   126â†’Wait for exact confirmation.
   127â†’
   128â†’If confirmed:
   129â†’```bash
   130â†’git checkout <base-branch>
   131â†’git branch -D <feature-branch>
   132â†’```
   133â†’
   134â†’Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 5)
   135â†’
   136â†’### Step 5: Cleanup Worktree
   137â†’
   138â†’**For Options 1, 2, 4:**
   139â†’
   140â†’Check if in worktree:
   141â†’```bash
   142â†’git worktree list | grep $(git branch --show-current)
   143â†’```
   144â†’
   145â†’If yes:
   146â†’```bash
   147â†’git worktree remove <worktree-path>
   148â†’```
   149â†’
   150â†’**For Option 3:** Keep worktree.
   151â†’
   152â†’## Quick Reference
   153â†’
   154â†’| Option | Merge | Push | Keep Worktree | Cleanup Branch |
   155â†’|--------|-------|------|---------------|----------------|
   156â†’| 1. Merge locally | âś“ | - | - | âś“ |
   157â†’| 2. Create PR | - | âś“ | âś“ | - |
   158â†’| 3. Keep as-is | - | - | âś“ | - |
   159â†’| 4. Discard | - | - | - | âś“ (force) |
   160â†’
   161â†’## Common Mistakes
   162â†’
   163â†’**Skipping test verification**
   164â†’- **Problem:** Merge broken code, create failing PR
   165â†’- **Fix:** Always verify tests before offering options
   166â†’
   167â†’**Open-ended questions**
   168â†’- **Problem:** "What should I do next?" â†’ ambiguous
   169â†’- **Fix:** Present exactly 4 structured options
   170â†’
   171â†’**Automatic worktree cleanup**
   172â†’- **Problem:** Remove worktree when might need it (Option 2, 3)
   173â†’- **Fix:** Only cleanup for Options 1 and 4
   174â†’
   175â†’**No confirmation for discard**
   176â†’- **Problem:** Accidentally delete work
   177â†’- **Fix:** Require typed "discard" confirmation
   178â†’
   179â†’## Red Flags
   180â†’
   181â†’**Never:**
   182â†’- Proceed with failing tests
   183â†’- Merge without verifying tests on result
   184â†’- Delete work without confirmation
   185â†’- Force-push without explicit request
   186â†’
   187â†’**Always:**
   188â†’- Verify tests before offering options
   189â†’- Present exactly 4 options
   190â†’- Get typed confirmation for Option 4
   191â†’- Clean up worktree for Options 1 & 4 only
   192â†’
   193â†’## Integration
   194â†’
   195â†’**Called by:**
   196â†’- **subagent-driven-development** (Step 7) - After all tasks complete
   197â†’- **executing-plans** (Step 5) - After all batches complete
   198â†’
   199â†’**Pairs with:**
   200â†’- **using-git-worktrees** - Cleans up worktree created by that skill
   201â†’

     2â†’name: brainstorming
     3â†’description: "You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation."
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs
     7â†’
     8â†’Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.
     9â†’
    10â†’Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.
    11â†’
    12â†’<HARD-GATE>
    13â†’Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
    14â†’</HARD-GATE>
    15â†’
    16â†’## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"
    17â†’
    18â†’Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change â€” all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.
    19â†’
    20â†’## Checklist
    21â†’
    22â†’You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:
    23â†’
    24â†’1. **Explore project context** â€” check files, docs, recent commits
    25â†’2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) â€” this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
    26â†’3. **Ask clarifying questions** â€” one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
    27â†’4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** â€” with trade-offs and your recommendation
    28â†’5. **Present design** â€” in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
    29â†’6. **Write design doc** â€” save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
    30â†’7. **Spec self-review** â€” quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
    31â†’8. **User reviews written spec** â€” ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
    32â†’9. **Transition to implementation** â€” invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan
    33â†’
    34â†’## Process Flow
    35â†’
    36â†’```dot
    37â†’digraph brainstorming {
    38â†’    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    39â†’    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    40â†’    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    41â†’    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    42â†’    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    43â†’    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    44â†’    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    45â†’    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    46â†’    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    47â†’    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    48â†’    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];
    49â†’
    50â†’    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    51â†’    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    52â†’    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    53â†’    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    54â†’    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    55â†’    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    56â†’    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    57â†’    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    58â†’    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    59â†’    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    60â†’    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    61â†’    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    62â†’    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
    63â†’}
    64â†’```
    65â†’
    66â†’**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.
    67â†’
    68â†’## The Process
    69â†’
    70â†’**Understanding the idea:**
    71â†’
    72â†’- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
    73â†’- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
    74â†’- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec â†’ plan â†’ implementation cycle.
    75â†’- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
    76â†’- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
    77â†’- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
    78â†’- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria
    79â†’
    80â†’**Exploring approaches:**
    81â†’
    82â†’- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
    83â†’- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
    84â†’- Lead with your recommended option and explain why
    85â†’
    86â†’**Presenting the design:**
    87â†’
    88â†’- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
    89â†’- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
    90â†’- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
    91â†’- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
    92â†’- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense
    93â†’
    94â†’**Design for isolation and clarity:**
    95â†’
    96â†’- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
    97â†’- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
    98â†’- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
    99â†’- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.
   100â†’
   101â†’**Working in existing codebases:**
   102â†’
   103â†’- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
   104â†’- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
   105â†’- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.
   106â†’
   107â†’## After the Design
   108â†’
   109â†’**Documentation:**
   110â†’
   111â†’- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
   112â†’  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
   113â†’- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
   114â†’- Commit the design document to git
   115â†’
   116â†’**Spec Self-Review:**
   117â†’After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:
   118â†’
   119â†’1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
   120â†’2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
   121â†’3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
   122â†’4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.
   123â†’
   124â†’Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review â€” just fix and move on.
   125â†’
   126â†’**User Review Gate:**
   127â†’After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:
   128â†’
   129â†’> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."
   130â†’
   131â†’Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.
   132â†’
   133â†’**Implementation:**
   134â†’
   135â†’- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
   136â†’- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.
   137â†’
   138â†’## Key Principles
   139â†’
   140â†’- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
   141â†’- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
   142â†’- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
   143â†’- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
   144â†’- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
   145â†’- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense
   146â†’
   147â†’## Visual Companion
   148â†’
   149â†’A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool â€” not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.
   150â†’
   151â†’**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
   152â†’> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"
   153â†’
   154â†’**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.
   155â†’
   156â†’**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**
   157â†’
   158â†’- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual â€” mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
   159â†’- **Use the terminal** for content that is text â€” requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions
   160â†’
   161â†’A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question â€” use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question â€” use the browser.
   162â†’
   163â†’If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
   164â†’`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`
   165â†’

     2â†’name: writing-plans
     3â†’description: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Writing Plans
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.
    11â†’
    12â†’Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."
    15â†’
    16â†’**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).
    17â†’
    18â†’**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
    19â†’- (User preferences for plan location override this default)
    20â†’
    21â†’## Scope Check
    22â†’
    23â†’If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans â€” one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.
    24â†’
    25â†’## File Structure
    26â†’
    27â†’Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.
    28â†’
    29â†’- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
    30â†’- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
    31â†’- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
    32â†’- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.
    33â†’
    34â†’This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.
    35â†’
    36â†’## Bite-Sized Task Granularity
    37â†’
    38â†’**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
    39â†’- "Write the failing test" - step
    40â†’- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
    41â†’- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
    42â†’- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
    43â†’- "Commit" - step
    44â†’
    45â†’## Plan Document Header
    46â†’
    47â†’**Every plan MUST start with this header:**
    48â†’
    49â†’```markdown
    50â†’# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan
    51â†’
    52â†’> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.
    53â†’
    54â†’**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]
    55â†’
    56â†’**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]
    57â†’
    58â†’**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]
    59â†’
    60â†’---
    61â†’```
    62â†’
    63â†’## Task Structure
    64â†’
    65â†’````markdown
    66â†’### Task N: [Component Name]
    67â†’
    68â†’**Files:**
    69â†’- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
    70â†’- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
    71â†’- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`
    72â†’
    73â†’- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**
    74â†’
    75â†’```python
    76â†’def test_specific_behavior():
    77â†’    result = function(input)
    78â†’    assert result == expected
    79â†’```
    80â†’
    81â†’- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**
    82â†’
    83â†’Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
    84â†’Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"
    85â†’
    86â†’- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**
    87â†’
    88â†’```python
    89â†’def function(input):
    90â†’    return expected
    91â†’```
    92â†’
    93â†’- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**
    94â†’
    95â†’Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
    96â†’Expected: PASS
    97â†’
    98â†’- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**
    99â†’
   100â†’```bash
   101â†’git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
   102â†’git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
   103â†’```
   104â†’````
   105â†’
   106â†’## No Placeholders
   107â†’
   108â†’Every step must contain the actual content an engineer needs. These are **plan failures** â€” never write them:
   109â†’- "TBD", "TODO", "implement later", "fill in details"
   110â†’- "Add appropriate error handling" / "add validation" / "handle edge cases"
   111â†’- "Write tests for the above" (without actual test code)
   112â†’- "Similar to Task N" (repeat the code â€” the engineer may be reading tasks out of order)
   113â†’- Steps that describe what to do without showing how (code blocks required for code steps)
   114â†’- References to types, functions, or methods not defined in any task
   115â†’
   116â†’## Remember
   117â†’- Exact file paths always
   118â†’- Complete code in every step â€” if a step changes code, show the code
   119â†’- Exact commands with expected output
   120â†’- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits
   121â†’
   122â†’## Self-Review
   123â†’
   124â†’After writing the complete plan, look at the spec with fresh eyes and check the plan against it. This is a checklist you run yourself â€” not a subagent dispatch.
   125â†’
   126â†’**1. Spec coverage:** Skim each section/requirement in the spec. Can you point to a task that implements it? List any gaps.
   127â†’
   128â†’**2. Placeholder scan:** Search your plan for red flags â€” any of the patterns from the "No Placeholders" section above. Fix them.
   129â†’
   130â†’**3. Type consistency:** Do the types, method signatures, and property names you used in later tasks match what you defined in earlier tasks? A function called `clearLayers()` in Task 3 but `clearFullLayers()` in Task 7 is a bug.
   131â†’
   132â†’If you find issues, fix them inline. No need to re-review â€” just fix and move on. If you find a spec requirement with no task, add the task.
   133â†’
   134â†’## Execution Handoff
   135â†’
   136â†’After saving the plan, offer execution choice:
   137â†’
   138â†’**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**
   139â†’
   140â†’**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration
   141â†’
   142â†’**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints
   143â†’
   144â†’**Which approach?"**
   145â†’
   146â†’**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
   147â†’- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
   148â†’- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review
   149â†’
   150â†’**If Inline Execution chosen:**
   151â†’- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
   152â†’- Batch execution with checkpoints for review
   153â†’

     2â†’name: requesting-code-review
     3â†’description: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Requesting Code Review
     7â†’
     8â†’Dispatch superpowers:code-reviewer subagent to catch issues before they cascade. The reviewer gets precisely crafted context for evaluation â€” never your session's history. This keeps the reviewer focused on the work product, not your thought process, and preserves your own context for continued work.
     9â†’
    10â†’**Core principle:** Review early, review often.
    11â†’
    12â†’## When to Request Review
    13â†’
    14â†’**Mandatory:**
    15â†’- After each task in subagent-driven development
    16â†’- After completing major feature
    17â†’- Before merge to main
    18â†’
    19â†’**Optional but valuable:**
    20â†’- When stuck (fresh perspective)
    21â†’- Before refactoring (baseline check)
    22â†’- After fixing complex bug
    23â†’
    24â†’## How to Request
    25â†’
    26â†’**1. Get git SHAs:**
    27â†’```bash
    28â†’BASE_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD~1)  # or origin/main
    29â†’HEAD_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
    30â†’```
    31â†’
    32â†’**2. Dispatch code-reviewer subagent:**
    33â†’
    34â†’Use Task tool with superpowers:code-reviewer type, fill template at `code-reviewer.md`
    35â†’
    36â†’**Placeholders:**
    37â†’- `{WHAT_WAS_IMPLEMENTED}` - What you just built
    38â†’- `{PLAN_OR_REQUIREMENTS}` - What it should do
    39â†’- `{BASE_SHA}` - Starting commit
    40â†’- `{HEAD_SHA}` - Ending commit
    41â†’- `{DESCRIPTION}` - Brief summary
    42â†’
    43â†’**3. Act on feedback:**
    44â†’- Fix Critical issues immediately
    45â†’- Fix Important issues before proceeding
    46â†’- Note Minor issues for later
    47â†’- Push back if reviewer is wrong (with reasoning)
    48â†’
    49â†’## Example
    50â†’
    51â†’```
    52â†’[Just completed Task 2: Add verification function]
    53â†’
    54â†’You: Let me request code review before proceeding.
    55â†’
    56â†’BASE_SHA=$(git log --oneline | grep "Task 1" | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')
    57â†’HEAD_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
    58â†’
    59â†’[Dispatch superpowers:code-reviewer subagent]
    60â†’  WHAT_WAS_IMPLEMENTED: Verification and repair functions for conversation index
    61â†’  PLAN_OR_REQUIREMENTS: Task 2 from docs/superpowers/plans/deployment-plan.md
    62â†’  BASE_SHA: a7981ec
    63â†’  HEAD_SHA: 3df7661
    64â†’  DESCRIPTION: Added verifyIndex() and repairIndex() with 4 issue types
    65â†’
    66â†’[Subagent returns]:
    67â†’  Strengths: Clean architecture, real tests
    68â†’  Issues:
    69â†’    Important: Missing progress indicators
    70â†’    Minor: Magic number (100) for reporting interval
    71â†’  Assessment: Ready to proceed
    72â†’
    73â†’You: [Fix progress indicators]
    74â†’[Continue to Task 3]
    75â†’```
    76â†’
    77â†’## Integration with Workflows
    78â†’
    79â†’**Subagent-Driven Development:**
    80â†’- Review after EACH task
    81â†’- Catch issues before they compound
    82â†’- Fix before moving to next task
    83â†’
    84â†’**Executing Plans:**
    85â†’- Review after each batch (3 tasks)
    86â†’- Get feedback, apply, continue
    87â†’
    88â†’**Ad-Hoc Development:**
    89â†’- Review before merge
    90â†’- Review when stuck
    91â†’
    92â†’## Red Flags
    93â†’
    94â†’**Never:**
    95â†’- Skip review because "it's simple"
    96â†’- Ignore Critical issues
    97â†’- Proceed with unfixed Important issues
    98â†’- Argue with valid technical feedback
    99â†’
   100â†’**If reviewer wrong:**
   101â†’- Push back with technical reasoning
   102â†’- Show code/tests that prove it works
   103â†’- Request clarification
   104â†’
   105â†’See template at: requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md
   106â†’
