     2â†’name: receiving-code-review
     3â†’description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Code Review Reception
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Code review requires technical evaluation, not emotional performance.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** Verify before implementing. Ask before assuming. Technical correctness over social comfort.
    13â†’
    14â†’## The Response Pattern
    15â†’
    16â†’```
    17â†’WHEN receiving code review feedback:
    18â†’
    19â†’1. READ: Complete feedback without reacting
    20â†’2. UNDERSTAND: Restate requirement in own words (or ask)
    21â†’3. VERIFY: Check against codebase reality
    22â†’4. EVALUATE: Technically sound for THIS codebase?
    23â†’5. RESPOND: Technical acknowledgment or reasoned pushback
    24â†’6. IMPLEMENT: One item at a time, test each
    25â†’```
    26â†’
    27â†’## Forbidden Responses
    28â†’
    29â†’**NEVER:**
    30â†’- "You're absolutely right!" (explicit CLAUDE.md violation)
    31â†’- "Great point!" / "Excellent feedback!" (performative)
    32â†’- "Let me implement that now" (before verification)
    33â†’
    34â†’**INSTEAD:**
    35â†’- Restate the technical requirement
    36â†’- Ask clarifying questions
    37â†’- Push back with technical reasoning if wrong
    38â†’- Just start working (actions > words)
    39â†’
    40â†’## Handling Unclear Feedback
    41â†’
    42â†’```
    43â†’IF any item is unclear:
    44â†’  STOP - do not implement anything yet
    45â†’  ASK for clarification on unclear items
    46â†’
    47â†’WHY: Items may be related. Partial understanding = wrong implementation.
    48â†’```
    49â†’
    50â†’**Example:**
    51â†’```
    52â†’your human partner: "Fix 1-6"
    53â†’You understand 1,2,3,6. Unclear on 4,5.
    54â†’
    55â†’âťŚ WRONG: Implement 1,2,3,6 now, ask about 4,5 later
    56â†’âś… RIGHT: "I understand items 1,2,3,6. Need clarification on 4 and 5 before proceeding."
    57â†’```
    58â†’
    59â†’## Source-Specific Handling
    60â†’
    61â†’### From your human partner
    62â†’- **Trusted** - implement after understanding
    63â†’- **Still ask** if scope unclear
    64â†’- **No performative agreement**
    65â†’- **Skip to action** or technical acknowledgment
    66â†’
    67â†’### From External Reviewers
    68â†’```
    69â†’BEFORE implementing:
    70â†’  1. Check: Technically correct for THIS codebase?
    71â†’  2. Check: Breaks existing functionality?
    72â†’  3. Check: Reason for current implementation?
    73â†’  4. Check: Works on all platforms/versions?
    74â†’  5. Check: Does reviewer understand full context?
    75â†’
    76â†’IF suggestion seems wrong:
    77â†’  Push back with technical reasoning
    78â†’
    79â†’IF can't easily verify:
    80â†’  Say so: "I can't verify this without [X]. Should I [investigate/ask/proceed]?"
    81â†’
    82â†’IF conflicts with your human partner's prior decisions:
    83â†’  Stop and discuss with your human partner first
    84â†’```
    85â†’
    86â†’**your human partner's rule:** "External feedback - be skeptical, but check carefully"
    87â†’
    88â†’## YAGNI Check for "Professional" Features
    89â†’
    90â†’```
    91â†’IF reviewer suggests "implementing properly":
    92â†’  grep codebase for actual usage
    93â†’
    94â†’  IF unused: "This endpoint isn't called. Remove it (YAGNI)?"
    95â†’  IF used: Then implement properly
    96â†’```
    97â†’
    98â†’**your human partner's rule:** "You and reviewer both report to me. If we don't need this feature, don't add it."
    99â†’
   100â†’## Implementation Order
   101â†’
   102â†’```
   103â†’FOR multi-item feedback:
   104â†’  1. Clarify anything unclear FIRST
   105â†’  2. Then implement in this order:
   106â†’     - Blocking issues (breaks, security)
   107â†’     - Simple fixes (typos, imports)
   108â†’     - Complex fixes (refactoring, logic)
   109â†’  3. Test each fix individually
   110â†’  4. Verify no regressions
   111â†’```
   112â†’
   113â†’## When To Push Back
   114â†’
   115â†’Push back when:
   116â†’- Suggestion breaks existing functionality
   117â†’- Reviewer lacks full context
   118â†’- Violates YAGNI (unused feature)
   119â†’- Technically incorrect for this stack
   120â†’- Legacy/compatibility reasons exist
   121â†’- Conflicts with your human partner's architectural decisions
   122â†’
   123â†’**How to push back:**
   124â†’- Use technical reasoning, not defensiveness
   125â†’- Ask specific questions
   126â†’- Reference working tests/code
   127â†’- Involve your human partner if architectural
   128â†’
   129â†’**Signal if uncomfortable pushing back out loud:** "Strange things are afoot at the Circle K"
   130â†’
   131â†’## Acknowledging Correct Feedback
   132â†’
   133â†’When feedback IS correct:
   134â†’```
   135â†’âś… "Fixed. [Brief description of what changed]"
   136â†’âś… "Good catch - [specific issue]. Fixed in [location]."
   137â†’âś… [Just fix it and show in the code]
   138â†’
   139â†’âťŚ "You're absolutely right!"
   140â†’âťŚ "Great point!"
   141â†’âťŚ "Thanks for catching that!"
   142â†’âťŚ "Thanks for [anything]"
   143â†’âťŚ ANY gratitude expression
   144â†’```
   145â†’
   146â†’**Why no thanks:** Actions speak. Just fix it. The code itself shows you heard the feedback.
   147â†’
   148â†’**If you catch yourself about to write "Thanks":** DELETE IT. State the fix instead.
   149â†’
   150â†’## Gracefully Correcting Your Pushback
   151â†’
   152â†’If you pushed back and were wrong:
   153â†’```
   154â†’âś… "You were right - I checked [X] and it does [Y]. Implementing now."
   155â†’âś… "Verified this and you're correct. My initial understanding was wrong because [reason]. Fixing."
   156â†’
   157â†’âťŚ Long apology
   158â†’âťŚ Defending why you pushed back
   159â†’âťŚ Over-explaining
   160â†’```
   161â†’
   162â†’State the correction factually and move on.
   163â†’
   164â†’## Common Mistakes
   165â†’
   166â†’| Mistake | Fix |
   167â†’|---------|-----|
   168â†’| Performative agreement | State requirement or just act |
   169â†’| Blind implementation | Verify against codebase first |
   170â†’| Batch without testing | One at a time, test each |
   171â†’| Assuming reviewer is right | Check if breaks things |
   172â†’| Avoiding pushback | Technical correctness > comfort |
   173â†’| Partial implementation | Clarify all items first |
   174â†’| Can't verify, proceed anyway | State limitation, ask for direction |
   175â†’
   176â†’## Real Examples
   177â†’
   178â†’**Performative Agreement (Bad):**
   179â†’```
   180â†’Reviewer: "Remove legacy code"
   181â†’âťŚ "You're absolutely right! Let me remove that..."
   182â†’```
   183â†’
   184â†’**Technical Verification (Good):**
   185â†’```
   186â†’Reviewer: "Remove legacy code"
   187â†’âś… "Checking... build target is 10.15+, this API needs 13+. Need legacy for backward compat. Current impl has wrong bundle ID - fix it or drop pre-13 support?"
   188â†’```
   189â†’
   190â†’**YAGNI (Good):**
   191â†’```
   192â†’Reviewer: "Implement proper metrics tracking with database, date filters, CSV export"
   193â†’âś… "Grepped codebase - nothing calls this endpoint. Remove it (YAGNI)? Or is there usage I'm missing?"
   194â†’```
   195â†’
   196â†’**Unclear Item (Good):**
   197â†’```
   198â†’your human partner: "Fix items 1-6"
   199â†’You understand 1,2,3,6. Unclear on 4,5.
   200â†’âś… "Understand 1,2,3,6. Need clarification on 4 and 5 before implementing."
   201â†’```
   202â†’
   203â†’## GitHub Thread Replies
   204â†’
   205â†’When replying to inline review comments on GitHub, reply in the comment thread (`gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments/{id}/replies`), not as a top-level PR comment.
   206â†’
   207â†’## The Bottom Line
   208â†’
   209â†’**External feedback = suggestions to evaluate, not orders to follow.**
   210â†’
   211â†’Verify. Question. Then implement.
   212â†’
   213â†’No performative agreement. Technical rigor always.
   214â†’

     5â†’
     6â†’# Writing Skills
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’**Writing skills IS Test-Driven Development applied to process documentation.**
    11â†’
    12â†’**Personal skills live in agent-specific directories (`~/.claude/skills` for Claude Code, `~/.agents/skills/` for Codex)** 
    13â†’
    14â†’You write test cases (pressure scenarios with subagents), watch them fail (baseline behavior), write the skill (documentation), watch tests pass (agents comply), and refactor (close loopholes).
    15â†’
    16â†’**Core principle:** If you didn't watch an agent fail without the skill, you don't know if the skill teaches the right thing.
    17â†’
    18â†’**REQUIRED BACKGROUND:** You MUST understand superpowers:test-driven-development before using this skill. That skill defines the fundamental RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle. This skill adapts TDD to documentation.
    19â†’
    20â†’**Official guidance:** For Anthropic's official skill authoring best practices, see anthropic-best-practices.md. This document provides additional patterns and guidelines that complement the TDD-focused approach in this skill.
    21â†’
    22â†’## What is a Skill?
    23â†’
    24â†’A **skill** is a reference guide for proven techniques, patterns, or tools. Skills help future Claude instances find and apply effective approaches.
    25â†’
    26â†’**Skills are:** Reusable techniques, patterns, tools, reference guides
    27â†’
    28â†’**Skills are NOT:** Narratives about how you solved a problem once
    29â†’
    30â†’## TDD Mapping for Skills
    31â†’
    32â†’| TDD Concept | Skill Creation |
    33â†’|-------------|----------------|
    34â†’| **Test case** | Pressure scenario with subagent |
    35â†’| **Production code** | Skill document (SKILL.md) |
    36â†’| **Test fails (RED)** | Agent violates rule without skill (baseline) |
    37â†’| **Test passes (GREEN)** | Agent complies with skill present |
    38â†’| **Refactor** | Close loopholes while maintaining compliance |
    39â†’| **Write test first** | Run baseline scenario BEFORE writing skill |
    40â†’| **Watch it fail** | Document exact rationalizations agent uses |
    41â†’| **Minimal code** | Write skill addressing those specific violations |
    42â†’| **Watch it pass** | Verify agent now complies |
    43â†’| **Refactor cycle** | Find new rationalizations â†’ plug â†’ re-verify |
    44â†’
    45â†’The entire skill creation process follows RED-GREEN-REFACTOR.
    46â†’
    47â†’## When to Create a Skill
    48â†’
    49â†’**Create when:**
    50â†’- Technique wasn't intuitively obvious to you
    51â†’- You'd reference this again across projects
    52â†’- Pattern applies broadly (not project-specific)
    53â†’- Others would benefit
    54â†’
    55â†’**Don't create for:**
    56â†’- One-off solutions
    57â†’- Standard practices well-documented elsewhere
    58â†’- Project-specific conventions (put in CLAUDE.md)
    59â†’- Mechanical constraints (if it's enforceable with regex/validation, automate itâ€”save documentation for judgment calls)
    60â†’
    61â†’## Skill Types
    62â†’
    63â†’### Technique
    64â†’Concrete method with steps to follow (condition-based-waiting, root-cause-tracing)
    65â†’
    66â†’### Pattern
    67â†’Way of thinking about problems (flatten-with-flags, test-invariants)
    68â†’
    69â†’### Reference
    70â†’API docs, syntax guides, tool documentation (office docs)
    71â†’
    72â†’## Directory Structure
    73â†’
    74â†’
    75â†’```
    76â†’skills/
    77â†’  skill-name/
    78â†’    SKILL.md              # Main reference (required)
    79â†’    supporting-file.*     # Only if needed
    80â†’```
    81â†’
    82â†’**Flat namespace** - all skills in one searchable namespace
    83â†’
    84â†’**Separate files for:**
    85â†’1. **Heavy reference** (100+ lines) - API docs, comprehensive syntax
    86â†’2. **Reusable tools** - Scripts, utilities, templates
    87â†’
    88â†’**Keep inline:**
    89â†’- Principles and concepts
    90â†’- Code patterns (< 50 lines)
    91â†’- Everything else
    92â†’
    93â†’## SKILL.md Structure
    94â†’
    95â†’**Frontmatter (YAML):**
    96â†’- Two required fields: `name` and `description` (see [agentskills.io/specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) for all supported fields)
    97â†’- Max 1024 characters total
    98â†’- `name`: Use letters, numbers, and hyphens only (no parentheses, special chars)
    99â†’- `description`: Third-person, describes ONLY when to use (NOT what it does)
   100â†’  - Start with "Use when..." to focus on triggering conditions
   101â†’  - Include specific symptoms, situations, and contexts
   102â†’  - **NEVER summarize the skill's process or workflow** (see CSO section for why)
   103â†’  - Keep under 500 characters if possible
   104â†’
   105â†’```markdown
   106â†’---
   107â†’name: Skill-Name-With-Hyphens
   108â†’description: Use when [specific triggering conditions and symptoms]
   109â†’---
   110â†’
   111â†’# Skill Name
   112â†’
   113â†’## Overview
   114â†’What is this? Core principle in 1-2 sentences.
   115â†’
   116â†’## When to Use
   117â†’[Small inline flowchart IF decision non-obvious]
   118â†’
   119â†’Bullet list with SYMPTOMS and use cases
   120â†’When NOT to use
   121â†’
   122â†’## Core Pattern (for techniques/patterns)
   123â†’Before/after code comparison
   124â†’
   125â†’## Quick Reference
   126â†’Table or bullets for scanning common operations
   127â†’
   128â†’## Implementation
   129â†’Inline code for simple patterns
   130â†’Link to file for heavy reference or reusable tools
   131â†’
   132â†’## Common Mistakes
   133â†’What goes wrong + fixes
   134â†’
   135â†’## Real-World Impact (optional)
   136â†’Concrete results
   137â†’```
   138â†’
   139â†’
   140â†’## Claude Search Optimization (CSO)
   141â†’
   142â†’**Critical for discovery:** Future Claude needs to FIND your skill
   143â†’
   144â†’### 1. Rich Description Field
   145â†’
   146â†’**Purpose:** Claude reads description to decide which skills to load for a given task. Make it answer: "Should I read this skill right now?"
   147â†’
   148â†’**Format:** Start with "Use when..." to focus on triggering conditions
   149â†’
   150â†’**CRITICAL: Description = When to Use, NOT What the Skill Does**
   151â†’
   152â†’The description should ONLY describe triggering conditions. Do NOT summarize the skill's process or workflow in the description.
   153â†’
   154â†’**Why this matters:** Testing revealed that when a description summarizes the skill's workflow, Claude may follow the description instead of reading the full skill content. A description saying "code review between tasks" caused Claude to do ONE review, even though the skill's flowchart clearly showed TWO reviews (spec compliance then code quality).
   155â†’
   156â†’When the description was changed to just "Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks" (no workflow summary), Claude correctly read the flowchart and followed the two-stage review process.
   157â†’
   158â†’**The trap:** Descriptions that summarize workflow create a shortcut Claude will take. The skill body becomes documentation Claude skips.
   159â†’
   160â†’```yaml
   161â†’# âťŚ BAD: Summarizes workflow - Claude may follow this instead of reading skill
   162â†’description: Use when executing plans - dispatches subagent per task with code review between tasks
   163â†’
   164â†’# âťŚ BAD: Too much process detail
   165â†’description: Use for TDD - write test first, watch it fail, write minimal code, refactor
   166â†’
   167â†’# âś… GOOD: Just triggering conditions, no workflow summary
   168â†’description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
   169â†’
   170â†’# âś… GOOD: Triggering conditions only
   171â†’description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
   172â†’```
   173â†’
   174â†’**Content:**
   175â†’- Use concrete triggers, symptoms, and situations that signal this skill applies
   176â†’- Describe the *problem* (race conditions, inconsistent behavior) not *language-specific symptoms* (setTimeout, sleep)
   177â†’- Keep triggers technology-agnostic unless the skill itself is technology-specific
   178â†’- If skill is technology-specific, make that explicit in the trigger
   179â†’- Write in third person (injected into system prompt)
   180â†’- **NEVER summarize the skill's process or workflow**
   181â†’
   182â†’```yaml
   183â†’# âťŚ BAD: Too abstract, vague, doesn't include when to use
   184â†’description: For async testing
   185â†’
   186â†’# âťŚ BAD: First person
   187â†’description: I can help you with async tests when they're flaky
   188â†’
   189â†’# âťŚ BAD: Mentions technology but skill isn't specific to it
   190â†’description: Use when tests use setTimeout/sleep and are flaky
   191â†’
   192â†’# âś… GOOD: Starts with "Use when", describes problem, no workflow
   193â†’description: Use when tests have race conditions, timing dependencies, or pass/fail inconsistently
   194â†’
   195â†’# âś… GOOD: Technology-specific skill with explicit trigger
   196â†’description: Use when using React Router and handling authentication redirects
   197â†’```
   198â†’
   199â†’### 2. Keyword Coverage
   200â†’
   201â†’Use words Claude would search for:
   202â†’- Error messages: "Hook timed out", "ENOTEMPTY", "race condition"
   203â†’- Symptoms: "flaky", "hanging", "zombie", "pollution"
   204â†’- Synonyms: "timeout/hang/freeze", "cleanup/teardown/afterEach"
   205â†’- Tools: Actual commands, library names, file types
   206â†’
   207â†’### 3. Descriptive Naming
   208â†’
   209â†’**Use active voice, verb-first:**
   210â†’- âś… `creating-skills` not `skill-creation`
   211â†’- âś… `condition-based-waiting` not `async-test-helpers`
   212â†’
   213â†’### 4. Token Efficiency (Critical)
   214â†’
   215â†’**Problem:** getting-started and frequently-referenced skills load into EVERY conversation. Every token counts.
   216â†’
   217â†’**Target word counts:**
   218â†’- getting-started workflows: <150 words each
   219â†’- Frequently-loaded skills: <200 words total
   220â†’- Other skills: <500 words (still be concise)
   221â†’
   222â†’**Techniques:**
   223â†’
   224â†’**Move details to tool help:**
   225â†’```bash
   226â†’# âťŚ BAD: Document all flags in SKILL.md
   227â†’search-conversations supports --text, --both, --after DATE, --before DATE, --limit N
   228â†’
   229â†’# âś… GOOD: Reference --help
   230â†’search-conversations supports multiple modes and filters. Run --help for details.
   231â†’```
   232â†’
   233â†’**Use cross-references:**
   234â†’```markdown
   235â†’# âťŚ BAD: Repeat workflow details
   236â†’When searching, dispatch subagent with template...
   237â†’[20 lines of repeated instructions]
   238â†’
   239â†’# âś… GOOD: Reference other skill
   240â†’Always use subagents (50-100x context savings). REQUIRED: Use [other-skill-name] for workflow.
   241â†’```
   242â†’
   243â†’**Compress examples:**
   244â†’```markdown
   245â†’# âťŚ BAD: Verbose example (42 words)
   246â†’your human partner: "How did we handle authentication errors in React Router before?"
   247â†’You: I'll search past conversations for React Router authentication patterns.
   248â†’[Dispatch subagent with search query: "React Router authentication error handling 401"]
   249â†’
   250â†’# âś… GOOD: Minimal example (20 words)
   251â†’Partner: "How did we handle auth errors in React Router?"
   252â†’You: Searching...
   253â†’[Dispatch subagent â†’ synthesis]
   254â†’```
   255â†’
   256â†’**Eliminate redundancy:**
   257â†’- Don't repeat what's in cross-referenced skills
   258â†’- Don't explain what's obvious from command
   259â†’- Don't include multiple examples of same pattern
   260â†’
   261â†’**Verification:**
   262â†’```bash
   263â†’wc -w skills/path/SKILL.md
   264â†’# getting-started workflows: aim for <150 each
   265â†’# Other frequently-loaded: aim for <200 total
   266â†’```
   267â†’
   268â†’**Name by what you DO or core insight:**
   269â†’- âś… `condition-based-waiting` > `async-test-helpers`
   270â†’- âś… `using-skills` not `skill-usage`
   271â†’- âś… `flatten-with-flags` > `data-structure-refactoring`
   272â†’- âś… `root-cause-tracing` > `debugging-techniques`
   273â†’
   274â†’**Gerunds (-ing) work well for processes:**
   275â†’- `creating-skills`, `testing-skills`, `debugging-with-logs`
   276â†’- Active, describes the action you're taking
   277â†’
   278â†’### 4. Cross-Referencing Other Skills
   279â†’
   280â†’**When writing documentation that references other skills:**
   281â†’
   282â†’Use skill name only, with explicit requirement markers:
   283â†’- âś… Good: `**REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:test-driven-development`
   284â†’- âś… Good: `**REQUIRED BACKGROUND:** You MUST understand superpowers:systematic-debugging`
   285â†’- âťŚ Bad: `See skills/testing/test-driven-development` (unclear if required)
   286â†’- âťŚ Bad: `@skills/testing/test-driven-development/SKILL.md` (force-loads, burns context)
   287â†’
   288â†’**Why no @ links:** `@` syntax force-loads files immediately, consuming 200k+ context before you need them.
   289â†’
   290â†’## Flowchart Usage
   291â†’
   292â†’```dot
   293â†’digraph when_flowchart {
   294â†’    "Need to show information?" [shape=diamond];
   295â†’    "Decision where I might go wrong?" [shape=diamond];
   296â†’    "Use markdown" [shape=box];
   297â†’    "Small inline flowchart" [shape=box];
   298â†’
   299â†’    "Need to show information?" -> "Decision where I might go wrong?" [label="yes"];
   300â†’    "Decision where I might go wrong?" -> "Small inline flowchart" [label="yes"];
   301â†’    "Decision where I might go wrong?" -> "Use markdown" [label="no"];
   302â†’}
   303â†’```
   304â†’
   305â†’**Use flowcharts ONLY for:**
   306â†’- Non-obvious decision points
   307â†’- Process loops where you might stop too early
   308â†’- "When to use A vs B" decisions
   309â†’
   310â†’**Never use flowcharts for:**
   311â†’- Reference material â†’ Tables, lists
   312â†’- Code examples â†’ Markdown blocks
   313â†’- Linear instructions â†’ Numbered lists
   314â†’- Labels without semantic meaning (step1, helper2)
   315â†’
   316â†’See @graphviz-conventions.dot for graphviz style rules.
   317â†’
   318â†’**Visualizing for your human partner:** Use `render-graphs.js` in this directory to render a skill's flowcharts to SVG:
   319â†’```bash
   320â†’./render-graphs.js ../some-skill           # Each diagram separately
   321â†’./render-graphs.js ../some-skill --combine # All diagrams in one SVG
   322â†’```
   323â†’
   324â†’## Code Examples
   325â†’
   326â†’**One excellent example beats many mediocre ones**
   327â†’
   328â†’Choose most relevant language:
   329â†’- Testing techniques â†’ TypeScript/JavaScript
   330â†’- System debugging â†’ Shell/Python
   331â†’- Data processing â†’ Python
   332â†’
   333â†’**Good example:**
   334â†’- Complete and runnable
   335â†’- Well-commented explaining WHY
   336â†’- From real scenario
   337â†’- Shows pattern clearly
   338â†’- Ready to adapt (not generic template)
   339â†’
   340â†’**Don't:**
   341â†’- Implement in 5+ languages
   342â†’- Create fill-in-the-blank templates
   343â†’- Write contrived examples
   344â†’
   345â†’You're good at porting - one great example is enough.
   346â†’
   347â†’## File Organization
   348â†’
   349â†’### Self-Contained Skill
   350â†’```
   351â†’defense-in-depth/
   352â†’  SKILL.md    # Everything inline
   353â†’```
   354â†’When: All content fits, no heavy reference needed
   355â†’
   356â†’### Skill with Reusable Tool
   357â†’```
   358â†’condition-based-waiting/
   359â†’  SKILL.md    # Overview + patterns
   360â†’  example.ts  # Working helpers to adapt
   361â†’```
   362â†’When: Tool is reusable code, not just narrative
   363â†’
   364â†’### Skill with Heavy Reference
   365â†’```
   366â†’pptx/
   367â†’  SKILL.md       # Overview + workflows
   368â†’  pptxgenjs.md   # 600 lines API reference
   369â†’  ooxml.md       # 500 lines XML structure
   370â†’  scripts/       # Executable tools
   371â†’```
   372â†’When: Reference material too large for inline
   373â†’
   374â†’## The Iron Law (Same as TDD)
   375â†’
   376â†’```
   377â†’NO SKILL WITHOUT A FAILING TEST FIRST
   378â†’```
   379â†’
   380â†’This applies to NEW skills AND EDITS to existing skills.
   381â†’
   382â†’Write skill before testing? Delete it. Start over.
   383â†’Edit skill without testing? Same violation.
   384â†’
   385â†’**No exceptions:**
   386â†’- Not for "simple additions"
   387â†’- Not for "just adding a section"
   388â†’- Not for "documentation updates"
   389â†’- Don't keep untested changes as "reference"
   390â†’- Don't "adapt" while running tests
   391â†’- Delete means delete
   392â†’
   393â†’**REQUIRED BACKGROUND:** The superpowers:test-driven-development skill explains why this matters. Same principles apply to documentation.
   394â†’
   395â†’## Testing All Skill Types
   396â†’
   397â†’Different skill types need different test approaches:
   398â†’
   399â†’### Discipline-Enforcing Skills (rules/requirements)
   400â†’
   401â†’**Examples:** TDD, verification-before-completion, designing-before-coding
   402â†’
   403â†’**Test with:**
   404â†’- Academic questions: Do they understand the rules?
   405â†’- Pressure scenarios: Do they comply under stress?
   406â†’- Multiple pressures combined: time + sunk cost + exhaustion
   407â†’- Identify rationalizations and add explicit counters
   408â†’
   409â†’**Success criteria:** Agent follows rule under maximum pressure
   410â†’
   411â†’### Technique Skills (how-to guides)
   412â†’
   413â†’**Examples:** condition-based-waiting, root-cause-tracing, defensive-programming
   414â†’
   415â†’**Test with:**
   416â†’- Application scenarios: Can they apply the technique correctly?
   417â†’- Variation scenarios: Do they handle edge cases?
   418â†’- Missing information tests: Do instructions have gaps?
   419â†’
   420â†’**Success criteria:** Agent successfully applies technique to new scenario
   421â†’
   422â†’### Pattern Skills (mental models)
   423â†’
   424â†’**Examples:** reducing-complexity, information-hiding concepts
   425â†’
   426â†’**Test with:**
   427â†’- Recognition scenarios: Do they recognize when pattern applies?
   428â†’- Application scenarios: Can they use the mental model?
   429â†’- Counter-examples: Do they know when NOT to apply?
   430â†’
   431â†’**Success criteria:** Agent correctly identifies when/how to apply pattern
   432â†’
   433â†’### Reference Skills (documentation/APIs)
   434â†’
   435â†’**Examples:** API documentation, command references, library guides
   436â†’
   437â†’**Test with:**
   438â†’- Retrieval scenarios: Can they find the right information?
   439â†’- Application scenarios: Can they use what they found correctly?
   440â†’- Gap testing: Are common use cases covered?
   441â†’
   442â†’**Success criteria:** Agent finds and correctly applies reference information
   443â†’
   444â†’## Common Rationalizations for Skipping Testing
   445â†’
   446â†’| Excuse | Reality |
   447â†’|--------|---------|
   448â†’| "Skill is obviously clear" | Clear to you â‰  clear to other agents. Test it. |
   449â†’| "It's just a reference" | References can have gaps, unclear sections. Test retrieval. |
   450â†’| "Testing is overkill" | Untested skills have issues. Always. 15 min testing saves hours. |
   451â†’| "I'll test if problems emerge" | Problems = agents can't use skill. Test BEFORE deploying. |
   452â†’| "Too tedious to test" | Testing is less tedious than debugging bad skill in production. |
   453â†’| "I'm confident it's good" | Overconfidence guarantees issues. Test anyway. |
   454â†’| "Academic review is enough" | Reading â‰  using. Test application scenarios. |
   455â†’| "No time to test" | Deploying untested skill wastes more time fixing it later. |
   456â†’
   457â†’**All of these mean: Test before deploying. No exceptions.**
   458â†’
   459â†’## Bulletproofing Skills Against Rationalization
   460â†’
   461â†’Skills that enforce discipline (like TDD) need to resist rationalization. Agents are smart and will find loopholes when under pressure.
   462â†’
   463â†’**Psychology note:** Understanding WHY persuasion techniques work helps you apply them systematically. See persuasion-principles.md for research foundation (Cialdini, 2021; Meincke et al., 2025) on authority, commitment, scarcity, social proof, and unity principles.
   464â†’
   465â†’### Close Every Loophole Explicitly
   466â†’
   467â†’Don't just state the rule - forbid specific workarounds:
   468â†’
   469â†’<Bad>
   470â†’```markdown
   471â†’Write code before test? Delete it.
   472â†’```
   473â†’</Bad>
   474â†’
   475â†’<Good>
   476â†’```markdown
   477â†’Write code before test? Delete it. Start over.
   478â†’
   479â†’**No exceptions:**
   480â†’- Don't keep it as "reference"
   481â†’- Don't "adapt" it while writing tests
   482â†’- Don't look at it
   483â†’- Delete means delete
   484â†’```
   485â†’</Good>
   486â†’
   487â†’### Address "Spirit vs Letter" Arguments
   488â†’
   489â†’Add foundational principle early:
   490â†’
   491â†’```markdown
   492â†’**Violating the letter of the rules is violating the spirit of the rules.**
   493â†’```
   494â†’
   495â†’This cuts off entire class of "I'm following the spirit" rationalizations.
   496â†’
   497â†’### Build Rationalization Table
   498â†’
   499â†’Capture rationalizations from baseline testing (see Testing section below). Every excuse agents make goes in the table:
   500â†’
   501â†’```markdown
   502â†’| Excuse | Reality |
   503â†’|--------|---------|
   504â†’| "Too simple to test" | Simple code breaks. Test takes 30 seconds. |
   505â†’| "I'll test after" | Tests passing immediately prove nothing. |
   506â†’| "Tests after achieve same goals" | Tests-after = "what does this do?" Tests-first = "what should this do?" |
   507â†’```
   508â†’
   509â†’### Create Red Flags List
   510â†’
   511â†’Make it easy for agents to self-check when rationalizing:
   512â†’
   513â†’```markdown
   514â†’## Red Flags - STOP and Start Over
   515â†’
   516â†’- Code before test
   517â†’- "I already manually tested it"
   518â†’- "Tests after achieve the same purpose"
   519â†’- "It's about spirit not ritual"
   520â†’- "This is different because..."
   521â†’
   522â†’**All of these mean: Delete code. Start over with TDD.**
   523â†’```
   524â†’
   525â†’### Update CSO for Violation Symptoms
   526â†’
   527â†’Add to description: symptoms of when you're ABOUT to violate the rule:
   528â†’
   529â†’```yaml
   530â†’description: use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
   531â†’```
   532â†’
   533â†’## RED-GREEN-REFACTOR for Skills
   534â†’
   535â†’Follow the TDD cycle:
   536â†’
   537â†’### RED: Write Failing Test (Baseline)
   538â†’
   539â†’Run pressure scenario with subagent WITHOUT the skill. Document exact behavior:
   540â†’- What choices did they make?
   541â†’- What rationalizations did they use (verbatim)?
   542â†’- Which pressures triggered violations?
   543â†’
   544â†’This is "watch the test fail" - you must see what agents naturally do before writing the skill.
   545â†’
   546â†’### GREEN: Write Minimal Skill
   547â†’
   548â†’Write skill that addresses those specific rationalizations. Don't add extra content for hypothetical cases.
   549â†’
   550â†’Run same scenarios WITH skill. Agent should now comply.
   551â†’
   552â†’### REFACTOR: Close Loopholes
   553â†’
   554â†’Agent found new rationalization? Add explicit counter. Re-test until bulletproof.
   555â†’
   556â†’**Testing methodology:** See @testing-skills-with-subagents.md for the complete testing methodology:
   557â†’- How to write pressure scenarios
   558â†’- Pressure types (time, sunk cost, authority, exhaustion)
   559â†’- Plugging holes systematically
   560â†’- Meta-testing techniques
   561â†’
   562â†’## Anti-Patterns
   563â†’
   564â†’### âťŚ Narrative Example
   565â†’"In session 2025-10-03, we found empty projectDir caused..."
   566â†’**Why bad:** Too specific, not reusable
   567â†’
   568â†’### âťŚ Multi-Language Dilution
   569â†’example-js.js, example-py.py, example-go.go
   570â†’**Why bad:** Mediocre quality, maintenance burden
   571â†’
   572â†’### âťŚ Code in Flowcharts
   573â†’```dot
   574â†’step1 [label="import fs"];
   575â†’step2 [label="read file"];
   576â†’```
   577â†’**Why bad:** Can't copy-paste, hard to read
   578â†’
   579â†’### âťŚ Generic Labels
   580â†’helper1, helper2, step3, pattern4
   581â†’**Why bad:** Labels should have semantic meaning
   582â†’
   583â†’## STOP: Before Moving to Next Skill
   584â†’
   585â†’**After writing ANY skill, you MUST STOP and complete the deployment process.**
   586â†’
   587â†’**Do NOT:**
   588â†’- Create multiple skills in batch without testing each
   589â†’- Move to next skill before current one is verified
   590â†’- Skip testing because "batching is more efficient"
   591â†’
   592â†’**The deployment checklist below is MANDATORY for EACH skill.**
   593â†’
   594â†’Deploying untested skills = deploying untested code. It's a violation of quality standards.
   595â†’
   596â†’## Skill Creation Checklist (TDD Adapted)
   597â†’
   598â†’**IMPORTANT: Use TodoWrite to create todos for EACH checklist item below.**
   599â†’
   600â†’**RED Phase - Write Failing Test:**
   601â†’- [ ] Create pressure scenarios (3+ combined pressures for discipline skills)
   602â†’- [ ] Run scenarios WITHOUT skill - document baseline behavior verbatim
   603â†’- [ ] Identify patterns in rationalizations/failures
   604â†’
   605â†’**GREEN Phase - Write Minimal Skill:**
   606â†’- [ ] Name uses only letters, numbers, hyphens (no parentheses/special chars)
   607â†’- [ ] YAML frontmatter with required `name` and `description` fields (max 1024 chars; see [spec](https://agentskills.io/specification))
   608â†’- [ ] Description starts with "Use when..." and includes specific triggers/symptoms
   609â†’- [ ] Description written in third person
   610â†’- [ ] Keywords throughout for search (errors, symptoms, tools)
   611â†’- [ ] Clear overview with core principle
   612â†’- [ ] Address specific baseline failures identified in RED
   613â†’- [ ] Code inline OR link to separate file
   614â†’- [ ] One excellent example (not multi-language)
   615â†’- [ ] Run scenarios WITH skill - verify agents now comply
   616â†’
   617â†’**REFACTOR Phase - Close Loopholes:**
   618â†’- [ ] Identify NEW rationalizations from testing
   619â†’- [ ] Add explicit counters (if discipline skill)
   620â†’- [ ] Build rationalization table from all test iterations
   621â†’- [ ] Create red flags list
   622â†’- [ ] Re-test until bulletproof
   623â†’
   624â†’**Quality Checks:**
   625â†’- [ ] Small flowchart only if decision non-obvious
   626â†’- [ ] Quick reference table
   627â†’- [ ] Common mistakes section
   628â†’- [ ] No narrative storytelling
   629â†’- [ ] Supporting files only for tools or heavy reference
   630â†’
   631â†’**Deployment:**
   632â†’- [ ] Commit skill to git and push to your fork (if configured)
   633â†’- [ ] Consider contributing back via PR (if broadly useful)
   634â†’
   635â†’## Discovery Workflow
   636â†’
   637â†’How future Claude finds your skill:
   638â†’
   639â†’1. **Encounters problem** ("tests are flaky")
   640â†’3. **Finds SKILL** (description matches)
   641â†’4. **Scans overview** (is this relevant?)
   642â†’5. **Reads patterns** (quick reference table)
   643â†’6. **Loads example** (only when implementing)
   644â†’
   645â†’**Optimize for this flow** - put searchable terms early and often.
   646â†’
   647â†’## The Bottom Line
   648â†’
   649â†’**Creating skills IS TDD for process documentation.**
   650â†’
   651â†’Same Iron Law: No skill without failing test first.
   652â†’Same cycle: RED (baseline) â†’ GREEN (write skill) â†’ REFACTOR (close loopholes).
   653â†’Same benefits: Better quality, fewer surprises, bulletproof results.
   654â†’
   655â†’If you follow TDD for code, follow it for skills. It's the same discipline applied to documentation.
   656â†’

     2â†’name: verification-before-completion
     3â†’description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Verification Before Completion
     7â†’
     8â†’## Overview
     9â†’
    10â†’Claiming work is complete without verification is dishonesty, not efficiency.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** Evidence before claims, always.
    13â†’
    14â†’**Violating the letter of this rule is violating the spirit of this rule.**
    15â†’
    16â†’## The Iron Law
    17â†’
    18â†’```
    19â†’NO COMPLETION CLAIMS WITHOUT FRESH VERIFICATION EVIDENCE
    20â†’```
    21â†’
    22â†’If you haven't run the verification command in this message, you cannot claim it passes.
    23â†’
    24â†’## The Gate Function
    25â†’
    26â†’```
    27â†’BEFORE claiming any status or expressing satisfaction:
    28â†’
    29â†’1. IDENTIFY: What command proves this claim?
    30â†’2. RUN: Execute the FULL command (fresh, complete)
    31â†’3. READ: Full output, check exit code, count failures
    32â†’4. VERIFY: Does output confirm the claim?
    33â†’   - If NO: State actual status with evidence
    34â†’   - If YES: State claim WITH evidence
    35â†’5. ONLY THEN: Make the claim
    36â†’
    37â†’Skip any step = lying, not verifying
    38â†’```
    39â†’
    40â†’## Common Failures
    41â†’
    42â†’| Claim | Requires | Not Sufficient |
    43â†’|-------|----------|----------------|
    44â†’| Tests pass | Test command output: 0 failures | Previous run, "should pass" |
    45â†’| Linter clean | Linter output: 0 errors | Partial check, extrapolation |
    46â†’| Build succeeds | Build command: exit 0 | Linter passing, logs look good |
    47â†’| Bug fixed | Test original symptom: passes | Code changed, assumed fixed |
    48â†’| Regression test works | Red-green cycle verified | Test passes once |
    49â†’| Agent completed | VCS diff shows changes | Agent reports "success" |
    50â†’| Requirements met | Line-by-line checklist | Tests passing |
    51â†’
    52â†’## Red Flags - STOP
    53â†’
    54â†’- Using "should", "probably", "seems to"
    55â†’- Expressing satisfaction before verification ("Great!", "Perfect!", "Done!", etc.)
    56â†’- About to commit/push/PR without verification
    57â†’- Trusting agent success reports
    58â†’- Relying on partial verification
    59â†’- Thinking "just this once"
    60â†’- Tired and wanting work over
    61â†’- **ANY wording implying success without having run verification**
    62â†’
    63â†’## Rationalization Prevention
    64â†’
    65â†’| Excuse | Reality |
    66â†’|--------|---------|
    67â†’| "Should work now" | RUN the verification |
    68â†’| "I'm confident" | Confidence â‰  evidence |
    69â†’| "Just this once" | No exceptions |
    70â†’| "Linter passed" | Linter â‰  compiler |
    71â†’| "Agent said success" | Verify independently |
    72â†’| "I'm tired" | Exhaustion â‰  excuse |
    73â†’| "Partial check is enough" | Partial proves nothing |
    74â†’| "Different words so rule doesn't apply" | Spirit over letter |
    75â†’
    76â†’## Key Patterns
    77â†’
    78â†’**Tests:**
    79â†’```
    80â†’âś… [Run test command] [See: 34/34 pass] "All tests pass"
    81â†’âťŚ "Should pass now" / "Looks correct"
    82â†’```
    83â†’
    84â†’**Regression tests (TDD Red-Green):**
    85â†’```
    86â†’âś… Write â†’ Run (pass) â†’ Revert fix â†’ Run (MUST FAIL) â†’ Restore â†’ Run (pass)
    87â†’âťŚ "I've written a regression test" (without red-green verification)
    88â†’```
    89â†’
    90â†’**Build:**
    91â†’```
    92â†’âś… [Run build] [See: exit 0] "Build passes"
    93â†’âťŚ "Linter passed" (linter doesn't check compilation)
    94â†’```
    95â†’
    96â†’**Requirements:**
    97â†’```
    98â†’âś… Re-read plan â†’ Create checklist â†’ Verify each â†’ Report gaps or completion
    99â†’âťŚ "Tests pass, phase complete"
   100â†’```
   101â†’
   102â†’**Agent delegation:**
   103â†’```
   104â†’âś… Agent reports success â†’ Check VCS diff â†’ Verify changes â†’ Report actual state
   105â†’âťŚ Trust agent report
   106â†’```
   107â†’
   108â†’## Why This Matters
   109â†’
   110â†’From 24 failure memories:
   111â†’- your human partner said "I don't believe you" - trust broken
   112â†’- Undefined functions shipped - would crash
   113â†’- Missing requirements shipped - incomplete features
   114â†’- Time wasted on false completion â†’ redirect â†’ rework
   115â†’- Violates: "Honesty is a core value. If you lie, you'll be replaced."
   116â†’
   117â†’## When To Apply
   118â†’
   119â†’**ALWAYS before:**
   120â†’- ANY variation of success/completion claims
   121â†’- ANY expression of satisfaction
   122â†’- ANY positive statement about work state
   123â†’- Committing, PR creation, task completion
   124â†’- Moving to next task
   125â†’- Delegating to agents
   126â†’
   127â†’**Rule applies to:**
   128â†’- Exact phrases
   129â†’- Paraphrases and synonyms
   130â†’- Implications of success
   131â†’- ANY communication suggesting completion/correctness
   132â†’
   133â†’## The Bottom Line
   134â†’
   135â†’**No shortcuts for verification.**
   136â†’
   137â†’Run the command. Read the output. THEN claim the result.
   138â†’
   139â†’This is non-negotiable.
   140â†’

     2â†’name: subagent-driven-development
     3â†’description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Subagent-Driven Development
     7â†’
     8â†’Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.
     9â†’
    10â†’**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history â€” you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.
    11â†’
    12â†’**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration
    13â†’
    14â†’## When to Use
    15â†’
    16â†’```dot
    17â†’digraph when_to_use {
    18â†’    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    19â†’    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    20â†’    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    21â†’    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    22â†’    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    23â†’    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];
    24â†’
    25â†’    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    26â†’    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    27â†’    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    28â†’    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    29â†’    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    30â†’    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
    31â†’}
    32â†’```
    33â†’
    34â†’**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
    35â†’- Same session (no context switch)
    36â†’- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
    37â†’- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
    38â†’- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)
    39â†’
    40â†’## The Process
    41â†’
    42â†’```dot
    43â†’digraph process {
    44â†’    rankdir=TB;
    45â†’
    46â†’    subgraph cluster_per_task {
    47â†’        label="Per Task";
    48â†’        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
    49â†’        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
    50â†’        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
    51â†’        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
    52â†’        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
    53â†’        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
    54â†’        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
    55â†’        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
    56â†’        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
    57â†’        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
    58â†’        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    59â†’    }
    60â†’
    61â†’    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    62â†’    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    63â†’    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    64â†’    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];
    65â†’
    66â†’    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    67â†’    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    68â†’    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    69â†’    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    70â†’    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    71â†’    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    72â†’    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    73â†’    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    74â†’    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    75â†’    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    76â†’    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    77â†’    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    78â†’    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    79â†’    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    80â†’    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    81â†’    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    82â†’    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    83â†’    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
    84â†’}
    85â†’```
    86â†’
    87â†’## Model Selection
    88â†’
    89â†’Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.
    90â†’
    91â†’**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.
    92â†’
    93â†’**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.
    94â†’
    95â†’**Architecture, design, and review tasks**: use the most capable available model.
    96â†’
    97â†’**Task complexity signals:**
    98â†’- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec â†’ cheap model
    99â†’- Touches multiple files with integration concerns â†’ standard model
   100â†’- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding â†’ most capable model
   101â†’
   102â†’## Handling Implementer Status
   103â†’
   104â†’Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:
   105â†’
   106â†’**DONE:** Proceed to spec compliance review.
   107â†’
   108â†’**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.
   109â†’
   110â†’**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.
   111â†’
   112â†’**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
   113â†’1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
   114â†’2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
   115â†’3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
   116â†’4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human
   117â†’
   118â†’**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.
   119â†’
   120â†’## Prompt Templates
   121â†’
   122â†’- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
   123â†’- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
   124â†’- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent
   125â†’
   126â†’## Example Workflow
   127â†’
   128â†’```
   129â†’You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.
   130â†’
   131â†’[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
   132â†’[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
   133â†’[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]
   134â†’
   135â†’Task 1: Hook installation script
   136â†’
   137â†’[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
   138â†’[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]
   139â†’
   140â†’Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"
   141â†’
   142â†’You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"
   143â†’
   144â†’Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
   145â†’[Later] Implementer:
   146â†’  - Implemented install-hook command
   147â†’  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
   148â†’  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
   149â†’  - Committed
   150â†’
   151â†’[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
   152â†’Spec reviewer: âś… Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra
   153â†’
   154â†’[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
   155â†’Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.
   156â†’
   157â†’[Mark Task 1 complete]
   158â†’
   159â†’Task 2: Recovery modes
   160â†’
   161â†’[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
   162â†’[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]
   163â†’
   164â†’Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
   165â†’Implementer:
   166â†’  - Added verify/repair modes
   167â†’  - 8/8 tests passing
   168â†’  - Self-review: All good
   169â†’  - Committed
   170â†’
   171â†’[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
   172â†’Spec reviewer: âťŚ Issues:
   173â†’  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
   174â†’  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)
   175â†’
   176â†’[Implementer fixes issues]
   177â†’Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting
   178â†’
   179â†’[Spec reviewer reviews again]
   180â†’Spec reviewer: âś… Spec compliant now
   181â†’
   182â†’[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
   183â†’Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)
   184â†’
   185â†’[Implementer fixes]
   186â†’Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant
   187â†’
   188â†’[Code reviewer reviews again]
   189â†’Code reviewer: âś… Approved
   190â†’
   191â†’[Mark Task 2 complete]
   192â†’
   193â†’...
   194â†’
   195â†’[After all tasks]
   196â†’[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
   197â†’Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge
   198â†’
   199â†’Done!
   200â†’```
   201â†’
   202â†’## Advantages
   203â†’
   204â†’**vs. Manual execution:**
   205â†’- Subagents follow TDD naturally
   206â†’- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
   207â†’- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
   208â†’- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)
   209â†’
   210â†’**vs. Executing Plans:**
   211â†’- Same session (no handoff)
   212â†’- Continuous progress (no waiting)
   213â†’- Review checkpoints automatic
   214â†’
   215â†’**Efficiency gains:**
   216â†’- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
   217â†’- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
   218â†’- Subagent gets complete information upfront
   219â†’- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)
   220â†’
   221â†’**Quality gates:**
   222â†’- Self-review catches issues before handoff
   223â†’- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
   224â†’- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
   225â†’- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
   226â†’- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built
   227â†’
   228â†’**Cost:**
   229â†’- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
   230â†’- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
   231â†’- Review loops add iterations
   232â†’- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)
   233â†’
   234â†’## Red Flags
   235â†’
   236â†’**Never:**
   237â†’- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
   238â†’- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
   239â†’- Proceed with unfixed issues
   240â†’- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
   241â†’- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
   242â†’- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
   243â†’- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
   244â†’- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
   245â†’- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
   246â†’- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
   247â†’- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is âś…** (wrong order)
   248â†’- Move to next task while either review has open issues
   249â†’
   250â†’**If subagent asks questions:**
   251â†’- Answer clearly and completely
   252â†’- Provide additional context if needed
   253â†’- Don't rush them into implementation
   254â†’
   255â†’**If reviewer finds issues:**
   256â†’- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
   257â†’- Reviewer reviews again
   258â†’- Repeat until approved
   259â†’- Don't skip the re-review
   260â†’
   261â†’**If subagent fails task:**
   262â†’- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
   263â†’- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)
   264â†’
   265â†’## Integration
   266â†’
   267â†’**Required workflow skills:**
   268â†’- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
   269â†’- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
   270â†’- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
   271â†’- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks
   272â†’
   273â†’**Subagents should use:**
   274â†’- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task
   275â†’
   276â†’**Alternative workflow:**
   277â†’- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution
   278â†’

     9â†’
    10â†’When reviewing completed work, you will:
    11â†’
    12â†’1. **Plan Alignment Analysis**:
    13â†’   - Compare the implementation against the original planning document or step description
    14â†’   - Identify any deviations from the planned approach, architecture, or requirements
    15â†’   - Assess whether deviations are justified improvements or problematic departures
    16â†’   - Verify that all planned functionality has been implemented
    17â†’
    18â†’2. **Code Quality Assessment**:
    19â†’   - Review code for adherence to established patterns and conventions
    20â†’   - Check for proper error handling, type safety, and defensive programming
    21â†’   - Evaluate code organization, naming conventions, and maintainability
    22â†’   - Assess test coverage and quality of test implementations
    23â†’   - Look for potential security vulnerabilities or performance issues
    24â†’
    25â†’3. **Architecture and Design Review**:
    26â†’   - Ensure the implementation follows SOLID principles and established architectural patterns
    27â†’   - Check for proper separation of concerns and loose coupling
    28â†’   - Verify that the code integrates well with existing systems
    29â†’   - Assess scalability and extensibility considerations
    30â†’
    31â†’4. **Documentation and Standards**:
    32â†’   - Verify that code includes appropriate comments and documentation
    33â†’   - Check that file headers, function documentation, and inline comments are present and accurate
    34â†’   - Ensure adherence to project-specific coding standards and conventions
    35â†’
    36â†’5. **Issue Identification and Recommendations**:
    37â†’   - Clearly categorize issues as: Critical (must fix), Important (should fix), or Suggestions (nice to have)
    38â†’   - For each issue, provide specific examples and actionable recommendations
    39â†’   - When you identify plan deviations, explain whether they're problematic or beneficial
    40â†’   - Suggest specific improvements with code examples when helpful
    41â†’
    42â†’6. **Communication Protocol**:
    43â†’   - If you find significant deviations from the plan, ask the coding agent to review and confirm the changes
    44â†’   - If you identify issues with the original plan itself, recommend plan updates
    45â†’   - For implementation problems, provide clear guidance on fixes needed
    46â†’   - Always acknowledge what was done well before highlighting issues
    47â†’
    48â†’Your output should be structured, actionable, and focused on helping maintain high code quality while ensuring project goals are met. Be thorough but concise, and always provide constructive feedback that helps improve both the current implementation and future development practices.

1â†’# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template
     2â†’
     3â†’Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.
     4â†’
     5â†’```
     6â†’Task tool (general-purpose):
     7â†’  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
     8â†’  prompt: |
     9â†’    You are implementing Task N: [task name]
    10â†’
    11â†’    ## Task Description
    12â†’
    13â†’    [FULL TEXT of task from plan - paste it here, don't make subagent read file]
    14â†’
    15â†’    ## Context
    16â†’
    17â†’    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]
    18â†’
    19â†’    ## Before You Begin
    20â†’
    21â†’    If you have questions about:
    22â†’    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    23â†’    - The approach or implementation strategy
    24â†’    - Dependencies or assumptions
    25â†’    - Anything unclear in the task description
    26â†’

    28â†’

    30â†’
    31â†’    Once you're clear on requirements:
    32â†’    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    33â†’    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)

    35â†’    4. Commit your work
    36â†’    5. Self-review (see below)
    37â†’    6. Report back
    38â†’
    39â†’    Work from: [directory]
    40â†’
    41â†’    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    42â†’    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.
    43â†’
    44â†’    ## Code Organization
    45â†’
    46â†’    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
    47â†’    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
    48â†’    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
    49â†’    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
    50â†’    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
    51â†’      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS â€” don't split files on your own without plan guidance
    52â†’    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
    53â†’      and note it as a concern in your report
    54â†’    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
    55â†’      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.
    56â†’
    57â†’    ## When You're in Over Your Head
    58â†’
    59â†’    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
    60â†’    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.
    61â†’
    62â†’    **STOP and escalate when:**
    63â†’    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
    64â†’    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
    65â†’    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
    66â†’    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
    67â†’    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress
    68â†’
    69â†’    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
    70â†’    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
    71â†’    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
    72â†’    or break the task into smaller pieces.
    73â†’
    74â†’    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review
    75â†’
    76â†’    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:
    77â†’
    78â†’    **Completeness:**
    79â†’    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    80â†’    - Did I miss any requirements?
    81â†’    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?
    82â†’
    83â†’    **Quality:**

    87â†’

    96â†’    - Are tests comprehensive?
    97â†’
    98â†’    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.
    99â†’
   100â†’    ## Report Format
